question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Gases have a higher entropy than solids because the molecules in a gas are more disordered.
The entropy of a substance decreases when it transitions from a solid to a liquid.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as gases do have higher entropy than solids due to the greater disorder and freedom of movement of their molecules. Statement B is false because the entropy of a substance actually increases when it transitions from a solid to a liquid due to the increase in disorder and volume. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question: The temperature of a body of mass $m$ and specific heat capacity $s$ is raised slowly from $T_{1}$ to $T_{2}$. The change in entropy of the system is
explanation:
When heat is given to a mass, if its temperature changes, then its entropy is given by
$d s=\dfrac{d Q}{T}$
$\therefore \quad \int_{s_{i}}^{s_{f}} d s=\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} m s \dfrac{d T}{T}$
$s_{f}-s_{i}=m s(\ln (T))_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}$
$\Delta s=m s \ln \left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)$.
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A measure of degree of disorder of a system is known as
explanation:
A measure of degree of disorderness of a system is known as entropy.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Entropy of the universe tends to be
explanation:
Entropy of the universe increases, hence. it tends to be maximum.
So correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In an isolated system the entropy increases.

Reason: The process in an isolated system is adiabatic only.
explanation: An isolated system is capable of undergoing various processes, including (but not limited to) isothermal, adiabatic, cyclic, and others. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
A solid body of constant heat capacity $1\,J/^\circ C$ is being heated by keeping it in contact with reservoirs in two ways:
(i) Sequentially keeping in contact with 2 reservoirs such that each reservoir supplies same amount of heat.
(ii) Sequentially keeping in contact with 8 reservoirs such that each reservoir supplies same amount of heat. In both the cases body is brought from initial temperature $100^\circ C$ to final temperature $200^\circ C$. Entropy change of the body in the two cases respectively is:
explanation:
The entropy change of the body in the two cases is same as entropy is a state function.
So the option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Calculate the change in entropy of $n$ moles of a perfect gas when its temperature changes from $T_{1}$ to $T_{2}$ while its volume changes from $V_{1}$ to $V_{2}$ (Assume that $P$ is constant)
explanation:
$d S=\dfrac{d Q}{T}=\dfrac{d U+P d V}{T}=n C_{V} \dfrac{d T}{T}+n R \dfrac{d V}{V}$
$\therefore \quad {S_2} - {S_1} = n{C_V}\int\limits_{{T_1}}^{{T_2}} {\frac{{dT}}{T}} + nR\int\limits_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} {\frac{{dV}}{V}} $
$\Rightarrow \Delta S=n C_{V} \ln \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}+n R \ln \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$
subject: Physics

question:
Calculate the change in entropy when $1\,g$ of ice at $0^\circ C$ is heated to water at $40^\circ C$
$\left( {{L_{fus}} = 80\frac{{cal}}{g},{C_{water}} = \frac{{1\,g}}{{^\circ C}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\Delta S=\Delta S_{1}+\Delta S_{2}$
To melt ice + to rise the temperature
$ = \frac{{\Delta Q}}{T} + \int_{{T_1}}^{{T_2}} {\frac{{mcdT}}{T}} = \frac{{mL}}{T} + mc\int_{{T_1}}^{{T_2}} {\frac{{\Delta T}}{T}} $
$ = \frac{{1 \times 80}}{{273}} + 1 \times 1 \times 2.303\log \frac{{313}}{{273}}$
$ = 0.28 + 0.1366 = 0.42\,\,cal^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas is placed in a tank at $27^{\circ} {C}$. The pressure is initially $600\,k\,Pa$. One fourth of the gas is then released from the tank and thermal equilibrium is established. What will be the final pressure if the temperature is $327^{\circ} {C}$ ?
explanation:
As, we know that, the ideal gas equation is given as,
$p V=n R T$
It can be written as
$\frac{{{p_1}{V_1}}}{{{n_1}{T_1}}} = \frac{{{p_2}{V_2}}}{{{n_2}{T_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ $\quad(\because R=$ constant $)$
If one fourth of gas is released from tank,
then, $n_{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} n_{1}$ and $V_{2}=V_{1} \quad(\therefore$ No change $)$
Given, ${T_2} = 327 + 273 = 600\,k$
So, from Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{{600{V_1}}}{{{n_1}300}} = \frac{{{p_2}{V_1}}}{{\frac{3}{4}{n_1}600}} \Rightarrow {p_2} = 900\,k\,Pa$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: If $d Q, d U$ and $d W$ are heat energy absorbed, change in internal energy and external work done respectively by a diatomic gas at constant pressure, then $d W: d U: d Q$ is
explanation:
At constant pressure,Change in internal energy, $d U=n C_{v} d T$
Heat, $d Q=n C_{p} d T$
Work, $d W=P d V=n R d T$
$\therefore d W: d U: d Q=n R d T: n C_{v} d T: n C_{p} d T$
$=n R d T: n \dfrac{5}{2} R d T: n \dfrac{7}{2} R d T$
$=2: 5: 7$
subject: Physics

question: When $Q_{1}$ amount of heat is supplied to a monatomic gas at constant pressure, the work done by the gas is $W$. When $Q_{2}$ amount of heat is supplied to a diatomic gas at constant pressure, the work done by the gas is $2\,W$.Then $Q_{1}: Q_{2}=$
explanation:
At constant pressure,
$W=P \Delta V=n R \Delta T \Rightarrow W \propto \Delta T$
$\therefore \dfrac{W_{2}}{W_{1}}=\dfrac{(\Delta T)_{2}}{(\Delta T)_{1}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 W}{W}=\dfrac{(\Delta T)_{2}}{(\Delta T)_{1}}$
$\therefore(\Delta T)_{2}=2(\Delta T)_{1}$
For monoatomic gas, $f_{1}=3$
$\therefore Q_{1}=(\Delta U)_{1}+W_{1}=\dfrac{f_{1}}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{1}+W$
$\therefore Q_{1}=\dfrac{3}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{1}+n R(\Delta T)_{1}=\dfrac{5}{2} n R(\Delta T)$
For diatomic gas, $f_{2}=5$
$\therefore Q_{2}=(\Delta U)_{2}+W_{2}=\dfrac{f_{2}}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{2}+2 W$
$\Rightarrow Q_{2}=\dfrac{5}{2} n R \times 2(\Delta T)_{1}+2 n R(\Delta T)_{1}$
$\therefore Q_{2}=\dfrac{14}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{1}$
$\therefore \dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{5}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{1}}{\dfrac{14}{2} n R(\Delta T)_{1}}=5: 14$
subject: Physics

question: Match the following terms related to thermodynamics with their descriptions in columns and choose the correct option from codes given below.
explanation:
(A) Work $(W)$ is done by the system during an isothermal expansion, making A match with Q.
The Internal Energy $(\Delta U)$ (B) remains unchanged in an isothermal process for an ideal gas, so B matches with R.
Heat (Q) (C) in an isothermal process is equal to the work done by the gas, hence C matches with S.
The External Pressure (D) is only infinitesimally different from the system's pressure in a quasi-static process, thus D matches with P. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - Q,         B - P,  C - R,  D - S
A - P,  B - R,  C - Q,  D - S
A - R,  B - Q,  C - P,  D - S
A - Q,   B - R,  C - S,  D - P

subject: Physics

question: Match the following columns related to first law of Thermodynamics and choose the correct option from codes given below.
explanation:
The First Law of Thermodynamics (A) is essentially the conservation of energy principle applied to thermodynamic systems, making A matches with S.
Heat $(\Delta Q)$ (B) is the energy supplied to the system by its surroundings, so B matches with R.
Work $(\Delta W)$ (C) is the energy transfer that occurs when the system does work on its surroundings, hence C matches with Q.
The change in internal energy $(\Delta U)$ (D) is the part of the energy transfer that results in an increase in the system's internal energy, thus D matches with P. Correct option is (1).

options.text: A - S,         B - R,  C - Q,        D - P
A - P,  B - Q,   C - R,  D - S
A - R,  B - P,  C - S,  D - Q
A - Q,   B - S,  C - P,  D - R

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The change in internal energy $(\Delta U)$ of a system depends on the path taken to go from the initial to the final state.
The combination of heat supplied $(\Delta Q)$ and work done $(\Delta W)$ is path dependent, but their difference $(\Delta Q-\Delta W)$ is path - independent.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because the change in internal energy of a system is a state function and only depends on the initial and final states, not on the path taken. Statement B is true because while ( $\Delta Q$ ) and ( $\Delta W$ ) individually may depend on the path, their difference $\Delta Q-\Delta W=\Delta U$ is path-independent as it equates to the change in internal energy, which is a state function. Correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
If the internal energy of a system remains constant during a process $(\Delta U = 0),$ then the heat supplied to the system $(\Delta Q)$ is equal to the work done by the system $(\Delta W).$
The work done by a gas in moving a piston in a cylinder can be calculated by the equation $\Delta W=P \Delta V$, where $P$ is the pressure $\Delta V$ and is the change in volume.
explanation: Statement A is true as it correctly describes a case of the First Law of Thermodynamics when internal energy does not change $(\Delta Q=\Delta W)$.Statement B is also false and gives the formula for calculating the work done by a gas when it expands or compresses against a constant external pressure only. Its not applicable in every case. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Work can be considered a state variable in thermodynamics.
The internal energy of a system is always equal to the sum of the heat added to the system and the work done by the system.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because work, like heat, is not a state variable but a path dependent energy transfer. Statement B is also false because the internal energy change is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system, not plus. Correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Temperature determines the spontaneous direction of heat flow between two bodies in contact.
Heat and work are the only forms of energy transfer that can change the internal energy of a system.
explanation: Statement A is true as temperature gradients determine the direction of heat flow, from the higher to the lower temperature body. Statement B is also true because heat and work are the modes of energy transfer recognized in thermodynamics for changing a system's internal energy. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The state variables of a thermodynamic system in equilibrium can be divided into two categories: extensive and intensive variables.

Reason: Extensive variables depend on the size of the system, while intensive variables do not change when the system's size changes.
explanation: The assertion is correct as it accurately describes the classification of state variables. The reason is also correct and it explains why the assertion is true, making statement. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that the total energy supplied to a system is equal to the sum of the work done by the system and the change in its internal energy.

Reason: This is a statement of the conservation of energy, indicating that energy can neither be created nor destroyed, but only transferred or converted from one form to another.
explanation: The assertion is a correct statement of the First Law of Thermodynamics  $(\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W),$  which reflects the conservation of energy principle, stating that energy cannot be created or destroyed in an isolated system. The reason correctly explains the underlying principle of this law. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: An amount of $700\,J$ of heat is transferred to a diatomic gas allowing it to expand keeping the constant pressure. The work done on the gas is
explanation:
In an isobaric process,
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Heat absorbed by gas is
$\Delta Q=n C_{p} \Delta T$
$700=n\left(\dfrac{7}{2} R\right) \cdot \Delta T$
$\left[\because C_{p}=\dfrac{7}{2} R\right.$ for diatomic gas $]$
$ \Rightarrow n\Delta T = \frac{{700 \times 2}}{{7R}} = \frac{{200}}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
So, change in internal energy of gas,
$\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T=C_{V} n \Delta T$
$=\dfrac{5}{2} R \times \dfrac{200}{R}=500 {~J} \quad$ [from Eq.(2)]
So, from Eq. (1), work done is
$\Delta W=\Delta Q-\Delta U=700-500=200 {~J}$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Heat and work are modes of energy transfer to a system resulting in change in its internal energy.

Reason: Heat and work in thermodynamics are the state variables.
explanation:
In thermodynamic system, heat and work are not state variables because they depend on the path of connecting states, thus, heat and work are treated as a path functions in thermodynamics. According to first law of thermodynamics,
$Q=\Delta U+W$
$\Rightarrow \quad Q-W=\Delta U$
Thus, heat $Q$ and work $W$ are modes of energy transfer to a system resulting in change in its internal energy.
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Assertion is true, Reason is true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true, Reason is true but Reason is not the correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

subject: Physics

question: A gas is taken from state $A$ to state $B$ along two different paths 1 and 2. The heat absorbed and work done by the system along these two paths are $Q_{1}$ and $Q_{2}$ and $W_{1}$ and $W_{2}$ respectively. Then
explanation:
Internal energy is a state function, its change does not depend upon the path followed. Therefore change in internal energy is same, as initial and final states are same for both paths.
For path 1
By First Law of thermodynamics,
${Q_1} = \Delta U + {W_1} \Rightarrow {Q_1} - {W_1} = \Delta U\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For path 2
${Q_2} = \Delta U + {W_2} \Rightarrow {Q_2} - {W_2} = \Delta U{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
from (1) & (2)
$Q_{1}-W_{1}=Q_{2}-W_{2}$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A total of ${48 J}$ heat is given to one mole of helium kept in a cylinder. The temperature of helium increases by ${2^{\circ} {C}}$. The work done by the gas is (Given, ${R=8.3 {JK}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}}$.)
explanation:
${\Delta Q=48 {~J}, n=1, \Delta T=2^{\circ} {C}, R=8.3 {~J} / {K} {mol}}$
For Helium, ${C_{V}=\dfrac{3 R}{2}}$ and ${\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T}$
By using first law of thermodynamics,
${\Delta Q=\Delta U+W ; 48=n C_{V} \Delta T+W}$
${\Rightarrow 48=1 \times \dfrac{3 R}{2} \times 2+W}$
${\Rightarrow W=23.1 J}$
subject: Physics

question: First law of thermodynamics is a special case of
explanation:
According the first law of thermodynamics,$d Q=d U+d W$
This equation is the most general statement of the law of conservation of energy.
So correct option is (1)

question: The change in internal energy of $3\,mol$ of helium gas when its temperature is increased by ${2 K}$
explanation:
${\Delta U=\mu C_{\nu} d T=\mu \times \dfrac{3}{2} R d T}$
$=3 \times \dfrac{3}{2} \times 8.31 \times 2=74.8 {~J}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: First law of thermodynamics does not state about direction of flow of heat between two temperatures.

Reason: Heat supplied to a system is always equal to the increase in its internal energy.
explanation: The first law of thermodynamics focuses on the conversion of mechanical energy into heat energy and vice versa, without specifying the direction of heat flow $(\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W)$. Assertion is correct. Heat supplied to a gas can be converted into both interval energy change and work done. Reason is false. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The internal energy of a real gas is function of both temperature and volume.

Reason: Internal kinetic energy depends on temperature and internal potential energy depends on volume.
explanation: If it is ideal gas, $U \propto T$ only, But if it is real gas internal energy depends on both temperature (kinetic energy) and volume (potential energy). The reason correctly explains the factors influencing the internal energy. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

question:

Reason: Thermal energy is a thermodynamic variable.
explanation: The first law of thermodynamics is a restatement of the conservation of energy, stating that heat absorbed equals the increase in energy and the external work done during expansion. $P, V, T$ are thermodynamic variables But thermal energy $\Delta Q$ is not. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The heat supplied to a system is not always equal to the sum of increase in its internal energy and work done by the system.

Reason: When a system changes from one thermal equilibrium state to another, some heat is absorbed by it.
explanation: By first law of thermodynamics, stating that heat supplied to a system equals the increase in internal energy and the external work done $\left( {\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W} \right)$. Only, for isochoric process, (constant volume, $\Delta W=0$ ), all supplied heat increases internal energy. In other processes, heat addition can contribute to both internal energy and work. So, given are different facts. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: Unit mass of a liquid with volume $V_{1}$ is completely changed into a gas of volume $V_{2}$ at a constant external pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. If the latent heat of evaporation for the given mass is $L$, then the increase in the internal energy of the system is
explanation:
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+P \Delta V \Rightarrow m l=\Delta U+P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)$
$\Delta U=L-P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right) \quad(\because m=1)$.
subject: Physics

question: A system is taken from state $A$ to state $B$ along two different paths 1 and 2 . The heat absorbed and work done by the system along these two paths are $Q_{1}, Q_{2}$ and $W_{1}, W_{2}$ respectively then
explanation:
Internal energy change is path independent functions.
$\Delta U_{1}=\Delta U_{2}$
$Q_{1}-W_{1}=Q_{2}-W_{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: In a thermodynamic process, pressure of a fixed mass of a gas is changed in such a manner that the gas molecules gives out $20\,J$ of heat and $10\,J$ of work is done on the gas. If the initial internal energy of the gas was $40\,J$, then the final internal energy will be
explanation:
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W=\left(U_{f}-U_{i}\right)+\Delta W$
$ \Rightarrow dU = dQ - dW = - 20 - ( - 10) = - 10\,J$
${U_f} = {U_i} + \Delta U = 40 - 10 = 30\,J$.
subject: Physics

question: If $150\,J$ of heat is added to a system and the work done by the system is $110\,J$, then change in internal energy will be
explanation:
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta U = \Delta Q - \Delta W = 150 - 110 = 40\,J$
subject: Physics

question: In thermodynamic process, $200\,Joules$ of heat is given to a gas and $100\,Joules$ of work is also done on it. The change in internal energy of the gas is
explanation:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W;\Delta Q = 200\,J\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\Delta W = - 100\,J$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta U = \Delta Q - \Delta W = 2000 - (100) = 300\,J.$
subject: Physics

question: Work and heat are:
explanation: Work and heat are path functions.
subject: Physics

question: $200\,g$ water is heated from $40^\circ C$ to $60^\circ C$. Ignoring the slight expansion of water, the change in its internal energy is close to (Given specific heat of water $ = 4184\,J/kg\,K):$
explanation:
Volume of water does not change, no work is done on or by the system $(W=0)$
According to first law of thermodynamics
$Q=\Delta U+W$
For Isochoric process $Q=\Delta U$
$\Delta U = mc\Delta T = 2 \times 4184 \times 20 = 16.7\,k\,J.$
subject: Physics

question: Given that $1\,g$ of water in liquid phase has volume $1\,c{m^3}$ and vapour phase $1671\,c{m^3}$ at atmospheric pressure and the latent heat of vaporization of water is $2256\,J/g$; the change in the internal energy in joules for $1\,g$ of water at $373\,K$ when it changes from liquid phase to vapour phase at the same temperature is:
explanation:
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+W$
$\Delta U=\Delta Q-W$$\Delta U=m L-P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)$
$ = 1g\left( {2256\frac{J}{g}} \right) - {10^5} \times (1671 - 1) \times {10^{ - 6}}\,{m^3}$
$ = 2089$
subject: Physics

question: The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of $1\,g$ of Helium at NTP, from ${T_1}\,K$ to ${T_2}\,K$ is
explanation:
The amount of heat energy required,
$Q = \frac{f}{2}nR\Delta T$
$ \Rightarrow Q = \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{1}{4} \times {k_B}{N_a}\Delta T = \frac{3}{8}\,{N_a}{k_B}\left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: When heat energy of $1500\,J$ is supplied to a gas at constant pressure, $2.1 \times {10^5}N{m^{ - 2}}$ the change in its volume is $2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$. The increase in its internal energy is
explanation:
Heat supplied to a gas,
$\Delta Q = 1500J$
Work done by the gas,
$\Delta W = P\Delta V = 2.1 \times {10^5} \times {N^{ - 2}} \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$
$ = 5.25 \times {10^2}Nm = 525\,J$
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta Q=\Delta W+\Delta U$
$\Delta U=\Delta Q-\Delta W$
$ = 1500\,J - 525\,J = 975\,J$
subject: Physics

question: An electric heater supplies heat to a system at a rate of $120 \mathrm{~W}$. If system performs work at a rate of $80\,J{s^{ - 1}}$
explanation:
According to first Law of thermodynamic $\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{\Delta U}{\Delta t}+\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta t}$
Here, $\frac{{\Delta Q}}{{\Delta t}} = 120W,\frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta t}} = 80J{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta t}} = 120 - 80 = 40\,J{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: In a given process of an ideal gas, $dW = 0,\,\,dQ < 0$ then for the gas
explanation:
From first law of thermodynamics
$d Q=d U+d W$
$dQ = dU$ (Given $dw = 0$)
Since, $dQ < 0 \Rightarrow dU < 0$
Hence, temperature will decrease
subject: Physics

question: A sample of $0.1\,g$ of water at $100^\circ C$ and normal pressure $\left( {1.013 \times {{10}^5}\,N{m^{ - 2}}} \right)$ requires $54\,cal$ of heat energy to convert to steam at $100^\circ C$. If the volume of the steam produced is $167.1\,cc$, the change in internal energy of the sample, is
explanation:
Density of water $\rho = 1\,gm/cc$
$m = 0.1\,gm$
Initial Volume $ = 0.1\,c{m^3}$
Volume of steam $ = 167.1\,c{m^3}$
$W = P\Delta V = 1.013 \times {10^5} \times 167 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 16.9\,\,J$
The heat required is
$\Delta Q = 54\,cal = 54 \times 4.18 = 225.7$
$\Delta U = \Delta Q - W = 208.8\,J$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: We can not change the temperature of a body without giving (or taking) heat to (or from) it.


Reason: According to principle of conservation of energy, total energy of a system should remain conserved.
explanation: We can change the temperature of a body without giving (or taking) heat to (or from) it. For example in an adiabatic compression temperature rises and in an adiabatic expansion temperature falls, although no heat is given or taken from the system. Option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
First law of thermodynamics says that heat flows from lower temperature to higher temperature.
Heat supplied to a system is always equal to the increase in its internal energy.
explanation: First law of thermodynamics tells only about the conversion of mechanical energy into the heat energy and vice versa. $\Delta Q=\Delta U$ (isochoric process). So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Study the following statements.
I. $\Delta Q$ and $W$ are path independent
II. $\Delta Q-W$ is path dependent
III. When $\Delta U=0$, then heat supplied to the system is used entirely for doing work on the surrounding.
Choose the correct statement
explanation:
Since $U$ is a state function, $\Delta U$ depends only on the initial & final states and not on the path taken by the gas to go from one state to other. However, $\Delta Q$ and $W$, in general depend on the path taken to go from the initial to final states.
Thus, from the first law of thermodynamics, i.e.
$\Delta Q-W=\Delta U$
$\text { It is clear that } \Delta Q-W \text { is path }$independent.
Also, when $\Delta U=0 \Rightarrow \Delta Q=W$
Hence, heat supplied to the system is used up entirely for doing work on the surrounding.
So option (3) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following curves represent the variation of coefficient of volume expansion of an ideal gas at constant pressure?
explanation:
Coefficient of volume expansion of a gas is
${\alpha _V} = \frac{1}{V}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For $P=$ constant
$\frac{V}{T} = C({\rm{constant}})\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = C\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From the above three eq's
$\alpha_{V}=\dfrac{1}{T C}(C)=\dfrac{1}{T}$
The graph between ${\alpha _V}\,\,\& \,\,\frac{1}{T}$ is a straight line passing through origin so the correct graph is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A gas system is taken through the thermodynamic cyclic process $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$ as shown below. The amount of heat released by the system is
explanation:
In cyclic process, $\Delta U=0$
So, by first law,
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
or $\quad \Delta Q=\Delta W$
As cycle given is clockwise so, work done is positive. Hence $\Delta Q$ is positive that means heat is absorbed.
Now, $\Delta Q=\Delta W=$ Area of cycle
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times(2 V-V) \times(2 p-p)=\dfrac{1}{2} p V$
Hence, heat released $=-$ Heat absorbed
$=-\dfrac{1}{2} p V$
subject: Physics

question: The $p-V$ diagram shown below indicates two paths along which a sample of gas can be taken from state $A$ to state $B$. The energy equal to $5 p V$ in the form of heat is transferred, if the Path-1 is chosen. How much energy in the form of heat should be transferred, if Path-2 is chosen?
explanation:
Work done in a thermodynamic process
$=$ Area under $p-V$ graph
For process 1, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952668/preptracker/questions/q_110327_explanation_1764952667970.png Work done, $W_{1}=p \Delta V=p(3 V-V)=2 p V$
Let change of internal energy in process 1
(point $A$ to point $B$) is $U_{1}$. Then, by first law of thermodynamics, heat required is
$\Delta Q_{1}=\Delta U+\Delta W=U_{1}+W_{1}$
$\Rightarrow 5 p V=U_{1}+2 p V$
Now for process 2,
$\Rightarrow U_{1}=3 p V$
Change of internal energy $=U_{2}=U_{1}=3 p V$
work done for process 2 is
$W_{2}=$ Area under process 2
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 V \times \dfrac{1}{2} p+p \times 2 V=\dfrac{5}{2} p V$
Again, using first law of thermodynamics, heat
required in process 2 is
$\Delta Q_{2}=\Delta U_{2}+\Delta W_{2}=3 p V+\dfrac{5}{2} p V=\dfrac{11}{2} p V$
subject: Physics

question: Find total work done from $A$ to $E$.
explanation:
Work done $=$ Area under $P-V$ curve
For $A \rightarrow B=$ Gas is expanding $\quad(W=+v e)$$B \rightarrow C=\Delta V$ is 0
$C \rightarrow E=$ Gas is compressing ( $W=-v e$ )
$\therefore \quad W_{\text {net }}=W_{A B}+W_{B C}+W_{C E}$
$=P_{0}\left(3 V_{0}-2 V_{0}\right)+0+2 P_{0}\left(V_{0}-3 V_{0}\right)$
$=P_{0} V_{0}-4 P_{0} V_{0}$
$W_{\text {net }}=-3 P_{0} V_{0}$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: A thermodynamic system is taken from an original state ${A}$ to an intermediate state ${B}$ by a linear process as shown in the figure. It's volume is then reduced to the original value from to by an isobaric process. The total work done by the gas from ${A}$ to ${B}$ and ${B}$ to ${C}$ would be
explanation:
Total work done by the gas for the process ${A B C=}$ Area under curve ${A B}$-Area under curve ${B C=}$ Area under the curve ${\triangle A B C}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956801/preptracker/questions/q_38956_explanation_1764956801030.png
Work done ${=\dfrac{1}{2} \times A C \times B C}$
Where ${A C=8000-4000=4000}$ dyne ${/ {cm}^{2}}$
${=4000 \times \dfrac{10^{-5}}{10^{-4}}=400 {~N} / {m}^{2}}$
${B C=7-3=4 m^{3}}$
${\therefore \quad}$ Work done ${=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 400 \times 4=800 J}$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
A real gas within a closed chamber at ${27^{\circ} {C}, P\left({~N} / {m}^{2}\right)}$ undergoes the cyclic process as shown in figure. The gas obeys ${P V^{3}=R T}$ equation for the path ${A}$ to ${B}$. The net work done in the complete cycle is
$({\text{assuming }}\,R = 8\,J/mol\,K)$
explanation:
Given,
${P V^{3}=R T, T_{1}=27^{\circ} {C}=300 {~K}, R=8 {~J} / {molK}}$
The net work done in complete cycle is
${W_{net}} = {W_{AB}} + {W_{BC}} + {W_{CA}}$
${W_{AB}} = \int_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} P \cdot dV = \int_2^4 {\frac{{RT}}{{{V^3}}}} dV$
$ = RT\int_2^4 {{V^{ - 3}}} dV$
${W_{AB}} = \frac{{RT}}{{ - 2}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{V^2}}}} \right]_2^4 = \frac{{ - RT}}{2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{16}} - \frac{1}{4}} \right]$
$ = \frac{{8 \times 300}}{2}\left( {\frac{{4 - 1}}{{16}}} \right)$
${W_{AB}} = 225\,J{\rm{ }}({\rm{expansion}})$
${W_{BC}} = \int_4^2 P \cdot dV = 10\,(2 - 4)$
$ = - 20{\mkern 1mu} J\,{\mkern 1mu} ({\rm{compression}})$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956966/preptracker/questions/q_38950_explanation_1764956965543.png
${W_{C A}=0(}$ As ${\Delta V=0)}$
${W_{\text {net }}=W_{A B}+W_{B C}+W_{C A}=225-20=205 J}$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The heat absorbed by a system in going through the given cyclic process is
explanation:
In cyclic process, heat absorbed by the system is equal to the work done by the system.
i.e., ${\Delta Q=W(\because \Delta U=0)}$
Now, ${W=}$ area under the given circle
${\therefore W=\pi r^{2}}$
${\Rightarrow W=\pi \times\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2}\right) \times\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}\right)}$
$ \Rightarrow W = \pi \times \left( {\frac{{340 - 60}}{2}} \right) \times {10^3}$
$ \times \left( {\frac{{340 - 60}}{2}} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}$
${\Rightarrow W=\pi \times \dfrac{280 \times 280}{4} \times 10^{-3}=61.5 {~J}}$
${\therefore \Delta Q=W=61.5 J}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956765/preptracker/questions/q_38935_explanation_1764956764691.png
So, option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
Two moles of an ideal monoatomic gas are taken through a cycle $A B C A$ as shown in the $P T$ diagram in figure. During the process $A B$, pressure and temperature of the gas vary such that $P T=$ constant. If $T_{0}=300 {~K}$, and the heat released by the gas in the process $A B$ (in $kJ$).
$(R = 8.3\,J/mo{l^{ - K}})$
explanation:
For process $A B, P T=$ constant
$\left( {\frac{{{\text{Constant}}}}{T}} \right)V = nRT$
$or\,\,T = k\sqrt V (k\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{a}}\,\,{\text{constant)}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad P \sqrt{V}=$ constant or $P V^{1 / 2}=$ constant
Thus, molar specific heat of gas,$C=C_{v}+\dfrac{R}{1-\dfrac{1}{2}}=C_{v}+2 R$
$=\dfrac{3}{2} R+2 R=\dfrac{7}{2} R$
Heat supplied to gas is $\Delta Q_{A B}=2 C\left(T_{0}-2 T_{0}\right)=-2 C T_{0}$
$=-7 R T_{0}=-2100 R$
Hence heat released by the gas,
${\left( {\Delta {Q_{AB}}} \right)_{{\rm{released }}}} = 2100 \times 8.3$
$ = 17430\,J = 17.43\,kJ$
subject: Physics

question: A gas is taken from state-1 to state-2 along the path shown in figure. If 70 $cal$ of heat is extracted from the gas in the process, calculate the change in internal energy of the system (in $J$).
explanation:
We know work done by a gas is given by the area under $P V$-curve or the area between $P V$-curve and the volume axis. Generally, we take volume on $x$-axis while plotting $P V$-curve but in figure it is taken on $y$-axis thus the work done is given by the shaded area shown in figure. In this process volume of gas decreases thus work is done on the gas and it is given as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956062/preptracker/questions/q_38897_explanation_1764956062296.png $W = - \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times (2 + 5) \times {10^5}$
$ = - 52.5\,J$
It is given that heat extracted in the process is 70 cal, thus
$Q=-70\,\, {cal}=70 \times 4.2 {~J}=-294 {~J}$
Now from first law of thermodynamics, we have$Q=W+\Delta U$
or
$\Delta U=Q-W=(-294)-(-52.5)=-241.5 {~J}$Thus in the process internal energy of gas decreases by 241.5 $J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: One mole of a monoatomic ideal gas follows a process ${A B}$, as shown. The specific heat of the process is ${\dfrac{13 R}{6}}$. The value of ${n}$ on ${P}$-axis is
explanation:
$C = \frac{{13R}}{6} \Rightarrow {C_v} = \frac{3}{2}R$
$C = \frac{{\Delta Q}}{{\Delta T}} = \frac{{(\Delta U + \Delta W)}}{{\Delta T}}$
$ = \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta T}} + \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta T}} = {C_v} + \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta T}}$
So, ${\quad \dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta T}=C-C_{v}=\dfrac{13 R}{6}-\dfrac{3 R}{2}=\dfrac{2 R}{3}}$
$\Delta T = {T_2} - {T_1}$
$ = \frac{{\left( {n{P_0}} \right)\left( {5{V_0}} \right)}}{R} - \frac{{\left( {3{P_0}} \right){V_0}}}{R} = \frac{{{P_0}{V_0}}}{R}(5n - 3)$
$\Delta W = \frac{{\left( {5{V_0} - {V_0}} \right)\left( {n{P_0} + 3{P_0}} \right)}}{2} = 2{P_0}{V_0}(n + 3)$
$\frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta T}} = \frac{{2(n + 3)R}}{{(5n - 3)}} = \frac{{2R}}{3}$
On solving, ${n=6}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question:
The diagram given below represents $P - T$ curve of a cyclic process. If number of moles of the gas are $n = 3,$ the work done by the gas in the process is
(Consider $R = 8.32\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
During paths $BC$ and $DA,$
$P \propto T\,$
Thus, the paths $BC$ and $DA$ are isochoric.Work done during an isochoric process is zero.
$\therefore \quad {W_{BC}} = {W_{DA}} = 0$
${W_{AB}} = nR\Delta T = nR\left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right)$
$ = 3 \times 8.32 \times (573 - 373)$
$ = 4992\,J$
Similarly, ${W_{CD}} = 3 \times 8.32 \times (873 - 473)$
$ = 9984\,J$
$\therefore $ Total work done $ = {W_{AB}} + {W_{CD}} = 4992 + 9984$
$ = 14976\,J$
$ = 14.98\,kJ$
subject: Physics

question: Amongst the two paths that may be taken by a gas to go from a state $A$ to a state $C$, in process $AB$ (as shown in figure), $360\,J$ of heat is added to the system. In process $BC,140\,J$ of heat is added to the system. The heat absorbed by the system in the process $AC$ will be
explanation:
${Q_{ABC}} = {Q_{AC}} + {W_{ABCA}}\,$
In this case,
${W_{ABCA}} = $Area of $PV$ graph $ = \frac{1}{2} \times AB \times BC$
$\therefore (360 + 140) = {Q_{AC}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \left( {8 \times {{10}^4} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)$
$\therefore {Q_{AC}} = 500 - 80 = 420\,J$
subject: Physics

question:
A cyclic Process of an ideal gas is shown in the figure. Let $W_{a \rightarrow b}, W_{b \rightarrow c}, W_{c \rightarrow d}$ and $W_{d \rightarrow a}$
represents the work done by the system during the processes $a \rightarrow b, b \rightarrow c, c \rightarrow d$ and $d \rightarrow a$ respectively. Consider the following relations

I. $W_{a \rightarrow b}+W_{b \rightarrow c}+W_{c \rightarrow d}+W_{d \rightarrow a}>0$
II. $W_{a \rightarrow b}+W_{b \rightarrow c}+W_{c \rightarrow a}+W_{d \rightarrow a} < 0$
III. $W_{a \rightarrow b}+W_{c \rightarrow d}>0$
IV. $W_{b \rightarrow c}+W_{d \rightarrow a}=0 \quad$
Which of the above relations is/are true?
explanation:
In Carnot cycle, in order to obtain continuous supply of work, net work done
As, $W_{a \rightarrow b}$ is isothermal
$W_{b \rightarrow c}$ is adiabatic
$W_{c \rightarrow d}$ is isothermal (negative)
$W_{d \rightarrow a}$ is adiabatic (negative)
Net work done during the complete cycle,
$W = {W_{a \to b}} + {W_{b \to c}} + \left( { - {W_{c \to d}}} \right) + \left( { - {W_{d \to a}}} \right)$
$W=W_{a \rightarrow b}-W_{c \rightarrow d}$
$\left[\operatorname{as} W_{b \rightarrow c}=W_{d \rightarrow a}\right]$
i.e. $\quad W_{a \rightarrow b}+W_{c \rightarrow d}>0$
${W_{a \to b}} + {W_{b \to c}} + {W_{c \to d}} + {W_{d \to a}} > 0$
$\,\,\,\,{W_{b \to c}} = {W_{d \to a}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,{W_{b \to c}} + {W_{d \to a}} = 0$
subject: Physics

question: The efficiency of an ideal gas with adiabatic exponent $\gamma$ for the shown cyclic process would be
explanation:
As, we know $W=p \Delta V=n R \Delta T$
$W_{B C}=-n R\left(2 T_{0}-T_{0}\right)=-n R T_{0}$
and $W_{C A}=+2 n R T_{0} \ln 2$
Work done $=W_{C A}+W_{B C}$
$ = 2\,nR{T_0}\ln 2 - n\,R{T_0}$
$ = n\,R{T_0}(2\ln 2 - 1)$
Also, input heat
$\Delta Q_{B C}=n C_{p} \Delta T=\dfrac{n R \gamma T_{0}}{\gamma-1}$
Efficiency $=\dfrac{\text { Work }}{\text { Input heat }}=\dfrac{(2 \ln 2-1)}{\gamma /(\gamma-1)}$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas is taken round the cyclic process ${M N O M}$. The work done by the gas is

Work done for cyclic process is given by area of the loop.

${=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(3 V_{0}-V_{0}\right)\left(3 P_{0}-P_{0}\right)}$
${=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(2 V_{0}\right)\left(2 P_{0}\right)}$
${W=2 P_{0} V_{0}}$.

question:
An ideal gas after going through a series of four thermodynamic states in order, reaches the initial state against (cyclic process). The amounts of heat and work involved in these states are
${Q_1} = 6000\,J,{Q_2} = - 5500\,J$
${Q_3} = - 3000\,J,{Q_2} = 3500\,J$
${W_1} = 2500\,J,{W_2} = - 1000\,J$
${W_3} = - 1200\,J,{W_4} = x\,J$
The ratio of net work done by the gas to the total heat absorbed by the gas is $\eta$. The value of $x$ and $\eta$ are nearly
explanation:
$\Delta Q=Q_{1}+Q_{2}+Q_{3}+Q_{4}$
$=6000-5500-3000+3500$
$\Delta Q = 1000\,J$
Total work done by ideal gas, i.e.,
$\begin{aligned}W & =W_{1}+W_{2}+W_{3}+W_{4} \\& =2500-1000-1200+x=300+x\end{aligned}$
For cyclic process, $\Delta U=U_{f}-U_{i}=0$
So, from first law of thermodynamics,
i.e., $\Delta Q=\Delta U+W$
$\Delta Q=W \quad(\therefore \Delta U=0)$
We have, $1000=(300+x)$
i.e., $x=1000-300=700 \mathrm{~J}$
Efficiency of the cycle is defined as
$\eta=\dfrac{\text { Work done }}{\text { heat Input }}$
$=\dfrac{W}{Q_{1}+Q_{4}}=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{\left(Q_{1}+Q_{4}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{1000}{(6000+3500)}=\dfrac{1000}{9500} \times 100$
$=0.105 \times 100=10.5 \%$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The heat energy absorbed by a system in going through a cyclic process shown in figure is
explanation:
From $P-V$ diagram work done by system in cyclic process is equal to area of the loop then Heat absorbed = Workdone $=$ area of the loop.

question: If ${Q_{iaf}} = 80\,cal,{W_{iaf}} = 60\,cal$ and $W =  - 30\,cal$ for the curved path $f \rightarrow i$, then value of $Q$ for path $f \rightarrow i$, will be

question: An ideal gas is taken through a cyclic thermodynamical process through four steps. The amounts of heat involved in these steps are:${Q_1} = 5960\,J,{Q_2} =  - 5585\,J,{Q_3} =  - 2980\,J$, ${Q_4} = 3645\,J$ respectively. The corresponding works involved are ${W_1} = 2200\,J,{W_2} =  - 825\,J,{W_3} =  - 1100\,J$ and $W_{4}$ respectively. The value of $W_{4}$ is :
explanation:
$\Delta Q=Q_{1}+Q_{2}+Q_{3}+Q_{4}$
$ = 5960 - 5585 - 2980 + 3645 = 1040\,J.$
$\Delta W=W_{1}+W_{2}+W_{3}+W_{4}$
$=2200-825-1100+W_{4}$
For cyclic process,
$U_{f}=U_{i}$
$\Delta U=U_{f}=U_{i}=0$
From first law of thermodynamic
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$
$1040 = 0 + 275 + {W_4}$
$\therefore {W_4} = 765\,J.$

question: A thermodynamic system is taken through the cycle $P Q R S P$ process. The net work done by the system is
explanation:

question: The $P-V$ diagram of a system undergoing thermodynamic transformation is shown in figure. The work done by the system in going from $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ is $30\,J$, and $40\,J$ heat is given to the system. The change in internal energy between $A$ and $C$ is
explanation:
Since, work is done by the system, so it is positive, therefore,
$\Delta W = 30\,J$
Heat given to the system, $\Delta Q = 40\,J$
using first law of thermodynamics
$\Delta U = \Delta Q - \Delta W = 40 - 30 = 10\,J$.

question: A gas can be taken from \(A\) to \(B\) via two different processes \(A C B\) and \(A D B\). When path \(A C B\) is used \[60\;J\] of heat flows into the system and \[30\,J\] of work is done by the system. If path \(A D B\) is used work done by the system is \[10\,J\]. The heat flow into the system in path \(A D B\) is
explanation:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$
$\Delta U = \Delta Q - W$
${(\Delta U)_{ACB}} = {(\Delta U)_{ADB}}$
$ \Rightarrow 60 - 30 = \Delta Q - 10$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta Q = 40\,\,J$
subject: Physics

question: An engine operates by taking \(n\) moles of an ideal gas through the cycle ABCDA shown in figure. The thermal efficiency of the engine is: (Take \(C_{V}=1.5 R\), where \(\mathrm{R}\) is gas constant)
explanation:
The net work done in the cycle is equal to the area enclosed in the graph $\Rightarrow W_{\text {cycle }}=P_{0} V_{0}$
$Path\,AB$
$\begin{aligned}\Delta Q_{A B} & =\Delta U+W \\& =\dfrac{P_{2} V_{2}-P_{1} V_{1}}{\gamma^{2}-1}+0=\dfrac{P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{5}{3}-1}=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2} \\\Delta Q_{B C} & =\Delta U+W \\& =\dfrac{4 P_{0} V_{0}-2 P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{5}{3}-1}+2 P_{0} V_{0}=5 P_{0} V_{0}\end{aligned}$
In the paths $CD\& DA,{\mkern 1mu} \,\Delta Q$ is (-) ve $\eta = \frac{{{W_{cycle{\rm{ }}}}}}{{{Q_{absorbed{\rm{ }}}}}} = \frac{{{P_0}{V_0}}}{{\frac{{3{P_0}{V_0}}}{2} + 5{P_0}{V_0}}} = \frac{2}{{13}} = 0.15$
subject: Physics

question: For the \[P - V\] diagram given for an ideal gas, out of the following which one correctly represents the \[T - P\] diagram?
explanation:
From $P-V$ graph,
$P \propto \dfrac{1}{V}, P V=$ constant $\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=$ constant
The correct graph is option (3)
subject: Physics

question:
An ideal gas goes through a reversible cycle \(\mathrm{a} \rightarrow b \rightarrow c \rightarrow d\) has the V-T diagram shown below. Process \(d \rightarrow a\) and \(b \rightarrow c\) are adiabatic.

The corresponding \[P - V\] diagram for the process is (all figures are schematic and not drawn to scale):
explanation:
In $VT$ graph
ab-process: Isobaric, volume and temperature increases.
bc-Process : Adiabatic, volume increases and pressure decreases.
cd-process : Isobaric, volume decreases.
da-process: Adiabatic, volume decreases and pressure increases.
The above processes correctly represented in $P - V$ digram (2).
subject: Physics

question: A certain amount of gas is taken through a cyclic process (\[A{\rm{ }}B{\rm{ }}C{\rm{ }}D{\rm{ }}A\]) that has two isobars, one isochore and one isothermal. The cycle can be represented on a \[P - V\] indicator diagram as:
explanation:
$P - V$ indicator diagram for isobaric
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956311/preptracker/questions/q_38104_explanation_1764956310681.png
$\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ indicator diagram for isochoric process
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956311/preptracker/questions/q_38104_explanation_1764956311480.png
$\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ indicator diagram for isothermal process
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956312/preptracker/questions/q_38104_explanation_1764956312196.png
So the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: On a \[T - P\] diagram, two moles of ideal gas perform process \[AB\] and \[CD\]. If the work done by the gas in the process \[AB\] is two times the work done in the process \[CD\] then what is the value of \(T_{1} / T_{2}\) ?
explanation:
Let slope of line $AC$ is $m_{1} \&$ that of line $DB$ is $m_{2}$, then slope $(m)=\dfrac{T \text { coordinate }}{P \text { coordinate }}$
$\Rightarrow P$-coordinate $=\dfrac{T-\text { coordinate }}{m}$ $P_{A}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{m_{1}}, P_{B}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{m_{2}}, P_{C}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{m_{1}} \& P_{D}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{m_{2}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956305/preptracker/questions/q_38097_explanation_1764956304666.png
Given that ${W_{AB}} = 2{W_{CD}}$
$nR{T_1}\ln \frac{{{P_A}}}{{{P_B}}} = 2nR{T_2}\ln \frac{{{P_C}}}{{{P_D}}}$
$nR{T_1}\ln \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = 2nR{T_2}\ln \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}}\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = 2$
subject: Physics

question: A graph is drawn between coefficient of volume expansion of the gas \(\alpha\) and temperature \[T\] of a gas having \[n = 2\] and \(\gamma=1.5\). Given that \[VT = K\] where \[K\] is a constant. Find the area of the graph between \(|\alpha|\) and \[T\], w.r.to \[T\]- axis from \(\dfrac{T_{\mathrm{o}}}{3}\) to \(T_{\text {o }}\)
explanation:
Given that $\mathrm{VT}=\mathrm{K}$ on differentiating the equation
$V d T+T d V=0 \Rightarrow V d T=-T d V$
$\dfrac{d V}{d T}=\dfrac{-V}{T} \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{1}{V} \dfrac{d V}{d T}=\dfrac{-1}{T}$
Area $=\int|\alpha| d t=\int_{T_{0} / 3}^{T_{0}}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) d T=\ln (3)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between Coefficient of Volume Expansion and Temperature

question: A cylinder containing an ideal gas and closed by a movable piston is submerged in an ice water mixture. The piston is quickly pushed down from position (1) to position (2) (process \[AB\]). The piston is held at position (2) until the gas is again at \(0^{\circ} C\) (process \[BC\] ). The position is slowly raised back to position (1) process \[(CA)\]. Which of the following \[P - V\] diagrams will correctly represent the processes \[AB,BC\] and \[CA\] and the cycle \[ABCA\]?
explanation: The process $AB$ is a rapid compression and hence it can be treated as adiabatic compression. The process $BC$ is isochoric and the process $CA$ is isothermal expansion.
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal diatomic gas undergoes a transition from \[A\] to \[B\] along a path \[AB\] as shown in the figure. The change in internal energy of the gas during the transition is
explanation:
The change in internal energy of the gas
For diatomic gas $\gamma=\dfrac{7}{5}$
$\Delta U = \frac{{{P_2}\,{V_2} - {P_1}\,{V_1}}}{{\gamma - 1}} = \frac{{2 \times 6 - 4 \times 5}}{{\frac{7}{5} - 1}} = - 20\,kJ$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: A thermodynamic system is taken through the cycle \(A B C D\) as shown in the figure. Heat rejected by the gas during the cycle is
explanation:
From the first law of thermodynamics $Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
For cyclic process $\Delta U=0$
Hence $Q = W$
Work done $W = $ area under $P - V$ diagram
$ \Rightarrow Q = - 2\,PV.$
subject: Physics

question: A thermodynamic system undergoes a cyclic process \[ABC\] as shown in the diagram. The work done by the system per cycle is
explanation:
Work done $=$ Area under $P-V$ curve
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 300 \times 5 = 750\,J$
Since the work done by the system is in anticlockwise direction, work done will be negative i.e., $ - 750\,J$.
subject: Physics

question:
One mole of an ideal gas is taken from \[A\] to \[B\], from \[B\] to \[C\] and then back to \[A\]. The variation of its volume with temperature for that change is as shown.
Its pressure at \[A\] is \[{P_0}\], volume is \[{V_0}\]. Then, the internal energy
explanation:
The internal energy of an ideal gas is only dependent upon its temperature.
From the graph ${T_A} = {T_B} \Rightarrow {U_A} = {U_B}$
subject: Physics

question: A point on $P-V$ diagram represents

options.text: Work done on or by the system
Work done in a cyclic process

subject: Physics

question:
A gas is taken through the cycle \[A \to B \to C \to A\], as shown in the figure.
What is the net work done by the gas?
explanation:
Work done in $P - V$ diagram is equal to area enclosed in $P - V$ curve
$ \Rightarrow W = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 4 \times {10^5} = 1000\,\,J$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas is taken via path \(A B C A\) as shown in figure. The net workdone in the whole cycle is
explanation:
The work done in a $PV$ diagram is the area enclosed.
The work done $ = 1/2\left( {3\,{V_1} - {V_1}} \right) \cdot \left( {4{P_1} - {P_1}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow W = - 3{P_1}\,{V_1}$
If the direction of change is anti clockwise then work done is negative.
subject: Physics

question: A gas at pressure $6 \times {10^5}N{m^{ - 2}}$ and volume $1\,{m^3}$ and its pressure falls to $4 \times {10^5}N{m^{ - 2}}$ when its volume is $3\,{m^3}$. Given that the indicator diagram is a straight line, work done by the system is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955908/preptracker/questions/q_37797_explanation_1764955907562.png
Work done by the system $=$ area of rectangle $BCDE + $ area of $\Delta ABC$
$=4 \times 10^{5} \times 2+\dfrac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 2}{2}$
$W = 10 \times {10^5}\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: An ideal gas undergoes four different processes from the same initial state as shown in the figure below. Those processes are adiabatic, isothermal, isobaric and isochoric. The curve which represents the adiabatic process among \(1,2,3\) and 4 is
explanation:
1 : Isochoric $(\because \mathrm{V}=$ constant $)$
2: Adiabatic $\left(\because\right.$ non linear $P V^{r}=$ constant $)$
3: Isothermal $$(\because P \propto \frac{1}{V}\,\,{\text{with}}\,\,T = $$ constant )
4: Isobaric ($$\because P = $$ constant )
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: An ideal gas undergoes cyclic process \(A B C D A\) as shown in given \[P - V\] diagram. The amount of work done by the gas is
explanation:
Work done by the gas is equal to area of rectangle $A B C D A,=A B \times B C=\left(2 V_{0}\right) p_{0}=2 p_{0} V_{0}$
For anticlockwise direction work is (-)ve work
$\therefore$ Work done by the gas $=-2 p_{0} V_{0}$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas undergoes a thermodynamic cycle as shown in the figure. Which of the following graph represents the same cycle?
explanation:
${\rm{Path}}\,AB$
The path $AB$ passes through the origin $V=m T$ ($\mathrm{m}$- slope) $ \Rightarrow P = $ constant
$V \rightarrow$ increases, $\mathrm{T}$ - increases.
${\rm{Path}}\,BC$
The path $BC$ is isochoric and hence $V = $ constant $ \Rightarrow P = mT$ ($m$ - constant )
$T$ - decreases $ \Rightarrow P$ - decreases
${\rm{Path}}{\mkern 1mu} \,CA$
The path $CA$ is isothermal
$PV = $ constant
$V$ - decreases $ \Rightarrow P$ - increases
subject: Physics

question: The above \[P - V\] diagram represents the thermodynamic cycle of an engine, operating with an ideal monoatomic gas. The amount of heat, extracted from the source in a single cycle is :
explanation:
Consider the cyclic process $A B C D A$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957248/preptracker/questions/q_37642_explanation_1764957247827.png
${\rm{Path}}\,AB$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta U=\dfrac{P_{2} V_{2}-P_{1} V_{1}}{\gamma-1}=\dfrac{2 P_{0} V_{0}-P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{5}{3}-1} \\& \Delta U=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2}, W=0 \\& \Delta Q_{A B}=\Delta U+W=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2}\end{aligned}$
${\rm{Path}}\,BC$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta U=\dfrac{4 P_{0} V_{0}-2 P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{2}{3}}=3 P_{0} V_{0} \\& W=2 P_{0}\left(2 V_{0}-V_{0}\right)=2 P_{0} V_{0} \\& \Delta Q_{B C}=5 P_{0} V_{0}\end{aligned}$
${\rm{Path}}{\mkern 1mu} \,CD$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{2 P_{0} V_{0}-4 P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{2}{3}}=-3 P_{0} V_{0}$
$W = 0\,\,\Delta {Q_{CD}} = - 3{P_0}{V_0}$
${\rm{Path}}\,DA$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta U=\dfrac{P_{0} V_{0}-2 P_{0} V_{0}}{\dfrac{2}{3}}=\dfrac{-3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2} \\& W=-P_{0} V_{0} \\& \Delta Q_{D A}=\dfrac{-5 P_{0} V_{0}}{2}\end{aligned}$
In the paths $AB$ and $BC$ gas is absorbing the heat.
$\Delta Q_{\text {absorbed }}=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2}+5 P_{0} V_{0}=\dfrac{13 P_{0} V_{0}}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: In the following indicator diagram, the net amount of work done will be :
explanation: The clockwise cycle area is more than the area of anticlockwise cycle.
subject: Physics

question: The work done by the gas in the cyclic process is
explanation:
$\left|W_{\text {cycle }}\right|=$ Area of the cycle $=\pi r^{2}=\pi r_{V} r_{P}$
Pressure radius $\left( {{r_P}} \right) = \frac{{(20 - 10)}}{2}k\,Pa = 5k\,Pa$
Volume radius $\left( {{r_V}} \right) = \frac{{(40 - 10)}}{2}{m^3} = 15{m^3}$
$W_{\text {cycle }}=-75 \pi K J$
$W=(+) v e$ (clock wise direction when $P$ is on $y - $ axis)
$W=(-)$ ve (clock wise direction when $P$ is on $x$ - axis)
subject: Physics

question: \[P - V\] plots for two gases during adiabatic process are shown in the figure. Plots 1 and 2 should correspond respectively, to
explanation:
For an adiabatic process, $P{V^\gamma } = $ constant,
Differentiating, we have
$\gamma P{V^{\gamma - 1}} + \frac{{dP}}{{dV}}{V^\gamma } = 0$ or
$\frac{{dP}}{{dV}} = - \frac{{\gamma P}}{V}$
Thus, the slope of $P - V$ curve is proportional to $\gamma $. Now, for a diatomic gas, $\gamma ( = 7/5)$ is less then that for a monoatomic gas for which $\gamma = 5/3$. Therefore, the slope of the $P - V$ curve is less for a diatomic gas than for a monoatomic gas. Hence curve 1 corresponds to diatomic gas ans curve 2 to monoatomic gas. Thus the correct choice is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: The PV diagram of \[4{\rm{ }}gm\] of \[He\] gas for a certain process \[A\] to \[B\] is shown in the figure. What is the heat given to the gas during the process \[A\] to \[B\] ?
explanation:
Number of moles $n=\dfrac{4 g}{4 g}=1$
$\begin{aligned}& W_{A B}=P_{0} V_{0}+\dfrac{1}{2} P_{0} V_{0}=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{2} \\& \Delta U_{A B}=n c_{v} \Delta T=\dfrac{3}{2}\left(2 P_{0} 2 V_{0}-P_{0} V_{0}\right) \\& \Delta U_{A B}=\dfrac{9}{2} P_{0} V_{0} \\& \Delta Q=W+\Delta U_{A B}=6 P_{0} V_{0}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: Consider a process shown in the figure. During this process the work done by the system
explanation: As the volume is continuously increasing and the work of expansion is always positive, so the work done by the system continuously increases.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: An ideal gas is initially at temperature \(T\) and Volume \[V\]. Its volume is increased by \(\Delta V\) due to an increase in temperature \(\Delta T\), pressure remaining constant. The quantity \(\delta=\dfrac{\Delta V}{V \Delta T}\) varies with temperature as
explanation:
Given that $P = $ Constant
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V}{T}=K=$ constant $\Rightarrow \Delta V=K \Delta T$
$\delta=\dfrac{\Delta V}{V \Delta T}=\dfrac{1}{V}(K)=\dfrac{K}{V}=\dfrac{1}{T}$
The graph between $\delta = \frac{1}{T}$ and $T$ is hyperbolic.
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following graphs is not possible between heat capacity and temperature of an ideal gas?
explanation: When temperature remains constant then the process is isothermal. In isothermal process $C=\infty$. so graph (3) is not possible.
subject: Physics

question: A cyclic process \(A B C D\) is shown in the following \(\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}\) diagram. Which of the following curves represent the same process ?
explanation:
${\rm{Path}}\,AB$
The path $AB$ is isobaric
$P = $ constant $\Rightarrow \dfrac{V}{T}=$ constant
$V \rightarrow$ increases $\Rightarrow T \rightarrow$ increases
${\rm{Path}}\,BC$
The path $BC$ represents isothermal process
$T = $ constant
$P \rightarrow$ Decreasing $\Rightarrow V \rightarrow$ Increasing
${\rm{Path}}{\mkern 1mu} \,CD$
The path $CD$ is isochoric
$V = $ constant $\Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{T}=$ constant
$P \rightarrow$ Decreases $\Rightarrow T \rightarrow$ Decreases
The graph between $P$ and $T$ is a straight line passing through origin.
${\rm{Path}}\,DA$
The path $DA$ isothermal
$T = $ Constant, $P \rightarrow$ increases $\Rightarrow V \rightarrow$ Decreases
subject: Physics

question: One mole of a diatomic ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process \[ABC\] as shown in figure. The process \[BC\] is adiabatic. The temperatures at \[A,B\] and \[C\] are \[400\;K,800\;K\] and \[600\;K\] respectively. Choose the correct statement:
explanation:
In cyclic process, change in total internal energy is zero.
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta U_{\text {cyclic }}=0 \\& \Delta U_{B C}=n C_{V} \Delta T=1 \times \dfrac{5 R}{2} \Delta T\end{aligned}$
Where, $C_{V}=$ molar specific heat at constant volume.
For $BC,{\rm{ }}\Delta T = - 200K$
$\therefore \Delta U_{B C}=-500 R$

options.text: The change in internal energy in whole cyclic process in $250R$.
The change in internal energy in the process $CA$ is $700R$.
The change in internal energy in the process $AB$ is $350R$.
The change in internal energy in the process $BC$ is $500R$.

subject: Physics

question: Find the heat absorbed by a system in going through the cyclic process shown in the figure
explanation:
$a = \frac{{\left( {2 \times {{10}^5} - 1 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{2} = \frac{{{{10}^5}}}{2}\,N/{m^2}$
$b = \frac{{(200 - 100)}}{2}\,c{m^3} \Rightarrow b = 50\,c{m^3}$
${W_{cycle}} = - 2.5\,\pi {\mkern 1mu} joules$.
$W = ( - )$ ve As $P$ is on $x$-axis and the gas follows clockwise path.
${W_{cycle{\rm{ }}}} = {\rm{ }}Area{\rm{ }} = \pi ab,\Delta q = W\left( {\Delta {U_{cycle{\rm{ }}}} = 0} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: \(n\) ' moles of an ideal gas undergoes a process \[A{\rm{ }} \to B\] as shown in the figure. The maximum temperature of the gas during the process will be :
explanation:
Equation of line is
$P{V_0} + {P_0}V = 3{P_0}{V_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Also $P V=n R T$
From the above two eq's
$T=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V}{n R}-\dfrac{P_{0} V^{2}}{n R V_{0}}$
for $T_{\max }, \dfrac{d T}{d V}=0 \Rightarrow V=\dfrac{3 V_{0}}{2}, P=\dfrac{3 P_{0}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow T_{\max }=\dfrac{9 P_{0} V_{0}}{4 n R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Graphs between P and V (Non Cyclic)

question: The Carnot heat engine has   an efficiency of $50 \%$. The temperature of the sink is maintained at $500\,K$. To increase the efficiency upto $80 \%$, the increment in the source temperature is
explanation:
As we know that,
$\eta=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
$\therefore \quad \eta_{1}=\dfrac{50}{100}=1-\dfrac{500}{T_{1_{i}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{500}{T_{1_{i}}}=1-\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{500}{T_{1_{i}}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow T_{1_{i}}=1000 {~K}$
Similarly, $\quad \eta_{2}=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1_{f}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{80}{100}=1-\dfrac{500}{T_{1_{j}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{500}{T_{1_{f}}}=1-\dfrac{80}{100}=\dfrac{20}{100}=\dfrac{1}{5}$
$\Rightarrow T_{1_{f}}=2500 {~K}$
$\therefore$ Change in temperature, $\Delta T=T_{1_{f}}-T_{1_{i}}$
$=2500-1000=1500 {~K}$
subject: Physics

question: An engine performs $2000\,J$ of mechanical work and discards $4000\,J$ of heat each cycle. What is the thermal efficiency of the engine?
explanation:
Let heat supplied to the engine $=Q_{1}$
Since, $Q_{1}=Q_{2}+W=2000+4000=6000 {~J}$
Thermal efficiency, $\eta=\dfrac{W}{Q_{4}} \times 100 \%$
$=\dfrac{2000}{6000} \times 100 \%=33.3 \%$
subject: Physics

question: A Carnot engine absorbs heat from a reservoir maintained at a temperature of $1000\,K$. The engine rejects heat to a reservoir whose temperature is $T$. If the magnitude of the absorbed heat is $400\,J$ and work performed is $300\,J$, then the value of $T$ is
explanation:
Thermal efficiency of Carnot's engine is
$\eta=\dfrac{W}{Q_{1}}=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{300}{400}=1-\dfrac{T}{1000}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T}{1000}=1-\dfrac{3}{4}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{1000}{4}=250 K$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal heat engine exhausting heat at ${77^{\circ} {C}}$ is to have ${30 \%}$ efficiency. It must take heat at
explanation:
Given: ${\eta=30 \%=0.3}$
${T_{2}=77^{\circ} {C}=350 {~K}, T_{1}=}$ ?
Then ${\eta=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} \quad \therefore \quad 0.3=1-\dfrac{350}{T_{1}}}$
${\Rightarrow T_{1}=500 {~K}}$ or ${T_{1}=227^{\circ} {C}}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: The temperature of source and sink of a heat engine are $127^\circ C$ and $27^\circ C,$ respectively. An inventor claims its efficiency to be $26 \%$, then
explanation:
Efficiency of heat engine is
$\eta = 1 - \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}$
${\rm{or }}\,\eta = \frac{{{T_1} - {T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}$
Here, $\quad {T_1} = 273 + 127 = 400\,K$
${T_2} = 273 + 27 = 300\,K$
$\therefore \quad \eta = \frac{{400 - 300}}{{400}}$
$ = \frac{{100}}{{400}} = 0.25 = 25\,\% $
Hence, $26 \%$ efficiency is impossible for a given heat engine.

options.text: it is possible with high probability
it is possible with low probability

subject: Physics

question: If the efficiency of an engine is $50 \%$ and its work output is $500\,J,$  then find the value of input work.
explanation:
$\because$ Efficiency of heat engine is
$\eta=\dfrac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Heat input }} \times 100$
or $\dfrac{\eta}{100}=\dfrac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Heat input }}=\dfrac{500}{\text { Heat input }}$
Heat input $ = \frac{{500 \times 100}}{{50}} = 1000\,J$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A reversible engine working between $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ cannot have efficiency more than $20 \%$.

Reason: Under ideal conditions
$\eta=1-\left(T_{2} / T_{1}\right)$.
explanation:
${T_1} = 227 + 273 = 500\,K$
${T_2} = 127 + 273 = 400\,K$
$\eta_{\text {max }}=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=1-\dfrac{400}{500}=0.2$
$\eta_{\max }=20 \%$ (As this is maximum, Assertion is true). So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A reversible engine takes heat from a reservior at $527^\circ C$ and gives out to the sink at $127^\circ C$. The engine is required to perform useful mechanical work at the rate of $750\,W$. How many calories per second must the engine take from the reservoir.
explanation:
$\dfrac{W}{Q}=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=1-\dfrac{400}{800}=0.5$
As $\eta=0.5=\dfrac{750}{4.2 \times Q}$
$ \Rightarrow Q = 357.1\,cal{\rm{/}}\sec $
subject: Physics

question: A Carnot engine has an efficiency of $50 \%$ when its source is at a temperature $327^\circ {\rm{ }}C$. The temperature of the sink is:
explanation:
Efficiency of Carnot engine
$\% \eta=\left(1-\dfrac{T_{\text {sink }}}{T_{\text {source }}}\right) \times 100$
Given : ${T_{{\rm{source}}}} = 327^\circ C = 600K$
$ \Rightarrow 50 = \left( {1 - \frac{{{T_{{\rm{sink }}}}}}{{600}}} \right) \times 100$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = 1 - \frac{{{T_{{\rm{sink }}}}}}{{600}}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_{\sin k{\rm{ }}}} = 300\,K$
$ \Rightarrow $ [Temp. of sink on $^\circ C$ scale]
$ \Rightarrow t = 300 - 273 = {\rm{ }}27^\circ {\rm{ }}C.$
subject: Physics

question: An engine has an efficiency of $1 / 6$. When the temperature of sink is reduced by $62^\circ C$, its efficiency is doubled. Temperature of the source is,
explanation:
Efficiency of engine is given by, $\eta=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}} = 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
In second case,
$\frac{{{T_2} - 62}}{{{T_1}}} = 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{2}{6} = \frac{2}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Using Eq.(1),
${T_2} = 310\,K = 310^\circ C - 273^\circ C = 37^\circ C$
Hence, ${T_1} = \frac{6}{5}{T_2} = 372\,K = 99^\circ C$
Hence, temperature of source is $99^\circ C.$
subject: Physics

question: The efficiency of an ideal heat engine working between the freezing point and boiling point of water, is
subject: Physics

question:
For a given heat engine,
I. Efficiency can be 1 .
II. Conversion of heat into work can be done by heating the system by an external furnace, as in a steam engine.
Choose the incorrect statement.
explanation:
Efficiency of a heat engine,
$\eta=1-\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}$
When $Q_{2}=0$, then $\eta=1$.
However in practice, $\eta=1$ is never possible even if we can eliminate various kinds of losses associated with actual heat engines.
So, option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A steam engine delivers $5.4 \times {10^8}\,J$ of work per minute and services $3.6 \times {10^9}\,J$ of heat per minute from the boiler. What is the efficiency of the engine? How much heat is wasted per minute?
explanation:
Given, work done $(W) = 5.4 \times {10^8}\,J/\min $
Total heat energy taken from the boiler,
$Q = 3.6 \times {10^9}\,J/\min $
Efficiency of
$(\eta ) = \frac{W}{Q} \times 100 = \frac{{5.4 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3.6 \times {{10}^9}}} \times 100 = 15\,\% $
Heat wasted per minute $=Q-W$
$ = 3.6 \times {10^9} - 5.4 \times {10^8} \approx 3.1 \times {10^9}\,J/\min $
subject: Physics

question: A motor cycle engine delivers a power $10\,kW$, by consuming petrol at the rate of $2.4\,kg/hour$. If the calorific value of petrol is $35.5\,MJ/kg$, the rate of heat rejection by the exhaust is
explanation:
Heat received (or produced by the burning of petrol) in hour will be
$ = (2.4\,kg/{\rm{ }}hour{\rm{ }})(35.5\,MJ/kg)$
$ = 85.2 \times {10^6}\,J/hour$
$\therefore$ The rate at which heat is received
$ = \frac{{85.2 \times {{10}^6}\,J}}{{(3600\,s)}} = 2.37 \times {10^4}\,J/s = 23.7\,k\,W$
The rate of heat rejection $=$ rate at which heat is produced-rate at which work is done
$ = 23.7\,k\,W - 10\,k\,W = 13.7\,k\,W$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal heat engine working between temperature $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ has an efficinecy $\eta$, the new efficiency if both the source and sink temperature are doubled, will be
explanation:
In first case $\eta_{1}=\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
In second case $\eta_{2}=\dfrac{2 T_{1}-2 T_{2}}{2 T_{1}}=\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\eta$
subject: Physics

question: A steam engine delivers $5.4 \times {10^8}\,J$ of work per minute and takes $3.6 \times {10^9}\,J$ of heat per minute from the boiler. What is the efficiency of the engine?
explanation:
Here, $Q_{1}=$ Heat absorbed per minute
$Q_{2}=$ Heat rejected per minute
We know that $\eta \,\% = \frac{W}{{{Q_1}}} \times 100$
$\eta \% = \frac{{5.4 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{3.6 \times {{10}^9}J}} \times 100 = \frac{3}{{20}} \times 100 = 15\,\% $
subject: Physics

question: An ideal heat engine has an efficiency $\eta$. The coefficient of performance of the engine when driven backward will be
explanation:

$\Rightarrow \eta=\dfrac{\text { output energy }}{\text { input energy }}=\dfrac{10}{4.2 \times 2}>1$

question:
An ideal gas is taken through a cyclic thermodynamic process through four steps. The amount of heat involved in these steps are ${Q_1} = 5960\,J,\,{Q_2} = - 5585\,J,$
${Q_3} = - 2980\,J$ and ${Q_4} = 3645\,J$ respectively.
The efficiency of the cycle is
explanation:
From the given problem,
$\Delta Q=Q_{1}+Q_{2}+Q_{3}+Q_{4}$
$=5960-5585-2980+3645$
$\Delta Q = 9605 - 8565 = 1040\,J$
Efficiency of a cycle is defined as $\eta=\dfrac{\text { Network }}{\text { Input heat }}=\dfrac{\Delta W}{Q_{1}+Q_{4}}=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_{1}+Q_{4}}$
Putting $\Delta Q = 1040\,J$
and ${Q_2} + {Q_1} = 5960 + 3645 = 9605\,J$
$\therefore \quad \eta = \frac{{1040}}{{9605}} = 0.1082 = 10.82\,\% $
subject: Physics

question: Match the following columns and choose the correct option from codes given below.
explanation:
State Variables (A) describe the equilibrium states of systems, making A match with Q.
Extensive Variables (B) are properties such as internal energy, volume, and total mass, which get halved when the system is divided into two equal parts, hence B matches with R.
Intensive Variables (C) include properties such as pressure, temperature, and density, which remain unchanged when the system is divided, so C matches with S.
The equation of State (D) is the connection between state variables, exemplified by the ideal gas law for an ideal gas, thus D matches with P . Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P,  B - Q,  C - R,    D - S

question: Match the following columns related to heat, Internal energy and work and choose the correct option from codes given below.
explanation:
Temperature (A) determines the direction of heat flow between two bodies, making it match with S.
Heat (B) is the flow of energy that occurs due to a temperature difference between the system and its surroundings, so B matches with R.
Internal Energy (C) is the sum of the kinetic and potential energies of all molecules within a system, hence C matches with Q.
A thermodynamic State Variable (D) depends only on the state of the system and not on the path taken to reach that state, which describes Internal Energy, making D matches with P. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - Q, B - R,    C - P,   D - S
A - S,  B - Q,  C - R,  D - P
A - R,  B - S,  C - Q,  D - P

question:
Rigid vessel of negligible mass containing $3$ moles of hydrogen gas is set free from a rough inclined plane of length $\sqrt 3 \,km$ and angle of inclination $60^{\circ}$. One third of heat due to friction is absorbed by the hydrogen gas. If the vessel stops just before reaching the bottom of the incline, then change in temperature of the hydrogen gas is
(Take $R = 8.0\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}},{C_{di}} = \frac{5}{2}R$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956680/preptracker/questions/q_38811_explanation_1764956680280.png The loss in potential energy due to friction will be converted to heat.
Potential energy loss $=$ Heat loss due to friction
i.e., heat loss $ = mgh = mgl\sin \alpha $
As, the vessel is rigid,
$\Delta V = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta W = 0$
$\therefore\,\,\, \Delta Q = \Delta U$
Since, ${\frac{1}{3}^{{\text{rd }}}}$ of the loss is absorbed by gas,
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{3}mgl\sin \alpha = \frac{5}{2}nR\,(\Delta T)$
$\therefore \quad \Delta T = \frac{{2\,mg\,l\,\sin \,\alpha }}{{15\,nR}} = \frac{{2{M_0}\,gl\,\sin \,\alpha }}{{15R}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because n = \frac{m}{{{M_0}}}} \right)$
Where ${M_0} = $ molecular weight of ${H_2} = 2\,g$
$\therefore \quad \Delta T = \frac{{2 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 9.8 \times \sqrt 3 \times {{10}^3} \times \sqrt 3 }}{{15 \times 8.0 \times 2}}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 9.8}}{{5 \times 8.0}}$
$\therefore \quad \Delta T = 0.49^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A barometer tube has length $90\,cm.$ It contains some air above mercury. On some hot days when temperature is $50^\circ C$ and true atmospheric pressure is $75\,cm$ of $Hg,$ the reading of the mercury level is $74.8\,cm.$ If the reading is observed to be $75.1\,cm$ on some another day when temperature is $25^\circ C,$ then Find the true pressure on that day will be
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956669/preptracker/questions/q_38777_explanation_1764956668695.png ${P_1} = 75 - 74.8 = 0.2\,cm$ of $Hg$
${P_2} = \left( {P' - 75.1} \right)cm$ of $Hg$
The length of barometer tube above mercury level is equivalent to volume of air in the tube in both cases
$\therefore {V_1} = 90 - 74.8 = 15.2$ and ${V_2} = 14.9$
From equation of state, $\frac{{{P_1}{V_1}}}{{{T_1}}} = \frac{{{P_2}{V_2}}}{{{T_2}}}$
($\because$ number of moles of gas is constant in the barometer tube)
$\therefore \frac{{0.2 \times 15.2}}{{(50 + 273)}} = \frac{{{P_2} \times 14.9}}{{(25 + 273)}}$
$\therefore \quad {P_2} = \frac{{298 \times 0.2 \times 15.2}}{{323 \times 14.9}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{298 \times 152 \times 0.2}}{{323 \times 149}}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 8 \times 0.2}}{{17}}$
$ = \frac{{3.2}}{{17}}$
$\therefore \quad {P_2} = 0.188 = 0.19$
$\therefore \quad P' = 75.4 + 0.19 \approx 75.59\,cm$ of $Hg$
subject: Physics

question:
Choose the incorrect statement from the following.

S4 : A process, whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a colder object to a hotter object is impossible.
explanation:
Efficency of heat engine,
$\eta=1-\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
For $(\eta = 1)\quad \quad ({\theta _1} = \infty )$
$\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$ should zero that is either, ${T_2} = 0\,K$
or $T_{1}=\infty K$
As source at infinite temperature or sink at $0 K$ is not attainable.
Therefore, efficiency of heat engine is always less than 1 .

question: At $27^\circ C,$ a motor car tyre has pressure of  $2\,atm.$ The temperature at which the tyre suddenly burst will be (take, $\gamma_{\text {air }}=1.4$ )
explanation:
When a system undergoes a sudden change under condition that no exchange of heat takes place between the system and surroundings, then such a process is called adiabatic. So, a tyre bursting suddenly an adiabatic process.
$p^{1-\gamma} T^{\gamma}=\text { constant }$
where, $\gamma$ is ratio of specific heats, $p$ is pressure and $T$ is temperature.
$\therefore \,\,\,\,{\left( {\frac{{{p_2}}}{{{p_1}}}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}} = {\left( {\frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} \right)^\gamma }$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{0.4}} = {\left( {\frac{{{T_2}}}{{300}}} \right)^{1.4}}$
$\therefore 0.4\left[ {\log 1 - \log 2} \right]$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 1.4\left[ {\log {T_2} - \log 300} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{T_2} = 246.1\,\,\,K$
subject: Physics

question: The state of a thermodynamic system is represented by
explanation: In thermodynamics, the value of a state variable depends only on the particular state not on the path used to attain that state. Pressure $(p)$, volume $(V)$, temperature $(T)$ and mass $(m)$ are state variables.

question: $1\,g$ of a liquid is converted to vapour at $3 \times {10^5}\,Pa$ pressure. If $10 \%$ of the heat supplied is used for increasing the volume by $1600\,c{m^3}$ during this phase change, then the increase in internal energy in the process will be
explanation:
By First Law of thermodynamics $Q=\Delta U+W$.
Here $W = P \times \Delta V$
$ = 3 \times {10^5} \times 1600 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,J = 480\,J.$
$\therefore$ Heat supplied $Q = \frac{w}{{0.1}} = 4800\,J$.
$\therefore \Delta U=Q-W=(4800-480) J=4320 J$.
subject: Physics

question:
Match the Column I (quantity) with Column II (value) and select the correct answer from the codes given below.
A $1\,\,cal = $
B $p{\left( {\frac{{\Delta V}}{{\Delta T}}} \right)_p} = $
C Specific heat capacity of solids

question: The following figure shows a Carnot engine that works between temperatures $T_{1}=400 {~K}$ and $T_{2}=200 {~K}$ and drives a Carnot refrigeration that works between temperatures $T_{3}=350 {~K}$ and $T_{4}=250 {~K}$. The quantity $Q_{3} / Q_{1}$ will be
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958360/preptracker/questions/q_110332_explanation_1764958360189.png As we know that,
$\eta_{c}=\dfrac{Q_{1}-Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{T_{1}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{Q_{1}-Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\dfrac{400-200}{400}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$Q_{1}-Q_{2}=\dfrac{Q_{1}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad Q_{2}=Q_{1} / 2$
and $\eta_{c}=\dfrac{W}{Q_{1}}=\dfrac{Q_{1}-Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow W=Q_{1} / 2$
Coefficient of performance of refrigerator is given as
$COP = \frac{{{T_C}}}{{{T_H} - {T_C}}} = \frac{{{Q_C}}}{W} = \frac{{{Q_4}}}{{{Q_3} - {Q_4}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $T_{C}$ and $T_{H}$ are cold and hot temperature $i.e.$ similar to $T_{4}$ and $T_{3}$, respectively.
$\therefore \quad C O P=\dfrac{T_{4}}{T_{3}-T_{4}}=\dfrac{250}{350-250}=\dfrac{Q_{4}}{W}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{250}{100}=\dfrac{Q_{4}}{W}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{5}{2} = \frac{{{Q_4}}}{W}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Substituting $W=Q_{1} / 2$ in Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{5}{2}=\dfrac{2 Q_{4}}{Q_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow Q_{4} / Q_{1}=5 / 4 \Rightarrow Q_{4}=(5 / 4) Q_{1}$
Substituting in Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{250}{350-250}=\dfrac{250}{100}=\dfrac{5 / 4 Q_{1}}{Q_{3}-5 / 4 Q_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{5}{2}=\dfrac{5 / 4 Q_{1}}{Q_{3}-5 / 4 Q_{1}}$
$5 Q_{3}-\dfrac{25}{4} Q_{1}=\dfrac{10 Q_{1}}{4}$
$\Rightarrow 5 Q_{3}=\dfrac{35 Q_{1}}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{Q_{3}}{Q_{1}}=\dfrac{7}{4}=1.75$
subject: Physics
