question: An ideal gas of mass $m$ in a state $A$ goes to another state $B$ via three different processes as shown in figure. If $Q_{1}, Q_{2}$ and $Q_{3}$ denote the heat absorbed by the gas along the three paths, then
explanation:
Initial and final states are same in all the process.
Hence, $\Delta U=0$ is same for each case.
$\therefore \Delta Q=\Delta W$
Area enclosed by curve with volume,
$\because(\text { Area })_{1} < (\text { Area })_{2} < (\text { Area })_{3}$
$\therefore Q_{1} < Q_{2} < Q_{3}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In isothermal process, whole of the heat energy supplied to the body is converted into internal energy.


Reason: According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
explanation:
In an isothermal process, the temperature being constant, change in internal energy would be zero. Hence heat supplied would be utilised to do external mechanical work. So assertion is incorrect.
Reason is correct as the given equation represents first law of thermodynamics.
So, correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A perfect gas goes from state $A$ to state $B$ by absorbing $8 \times {10^5}\,J$ of heat and doing $6.5 \times {10^5}\,J$ of external work. It is now transferred between the same two states in another process in which it absorbs ${10^5}\,J$ of heat. In the second process,
explanation:
According to first law of thermodynamics,
$d U=d Q-d W$
$dU = 8 \times {10^5} - 6.5 \times {10^5} = 1.5 \times {10^5}\,J$
In the second process, $d U$ remains the same.
$\therefore d W=d Q-d U$
$=1 \times 10^{5}-1.5 \times 10^{5}$
$dW = - 0.5 \times {10^5}\,J \simeq 0.5 \times {10^5}\,J$
$\therefore$ Work done on the gas is $0.5 \times {10^5}\,J$

options.text: Work done on the gas is ${10^5}\,J$
Work done on the gas is $0.5 \times {10^5}\,J$
Work done by the gas is ${10^5}\,J$
Work done by the gas is $0.5 \times {10^5}\,J$

subject: Physics

question: Two rigid boxes containing different ideal gases are placed on table. Box $A$ contains one mole of nitrogen at temperature $T_{0}$, while box $B$ contains one mole of helium at temperature $(7 / 3) T_{0}$. The boxes are then put into thermal contact with each other, and heat flows between them until the gases reach a common final temperature (ignore the heat capacity of boxes). Then, the final temperature of the gases $T_{f}$ in terms of $T_{0}$ is
explanation:
The system of two rigid boxes is shown in figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956041/preptracker/questions/q_38290_explanation_1764956040513.png We know that, $\Delta U=\dfrac{1}{2} f n R \Delta T$
$n=1$
$f=5$ for nitrogen and $f=3$ for helium
Change in internal energy at box $A$,
i.e. $\Delta U_{A}=1 \times \dfrac{5 R}{2}\left(T_{f}-T_{0}\right)$
Change in internal energy at box $B$,
i.e. $\Delta U_{B}=1 \times \dfrac{3 R}{2}\left(T_{f}-\dfrac{7}{3} T_{0}\right)$
Now, $\Delta U_{A}+\Delta U_{B}=0$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{5 R}{2}\left(T_{f}-T_{0}\right)+\dfrac{3 R}{2}\left(T_{f}-\dfrac{7 T_{0}}{3}\right)=0 \\& 5 T_{f}-5 T_{0}+3 T_{f}-7 T_{0}=0 \\& \Rightarrow 8 T_{f}=12 T_{0} \\& \Rightarrow T_{f}=\dfrac{12}{8} T_{0}=\dfrac{3}{2} T_{0}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A litre of dry air at STP expands adiabatically to volume of 3 litres. If $\gamma=1.40$, the work done by air is $\left(3^{1.4}=4.6555\right)$ [Take air to be an ideal gas]
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas is compressed isothermally in a rigid vessel to double its pressure at room temperature, $27^\circ C$. The work done on the gas will be:
explanation: Work done on gas $=n R T \ln \left(\dfrac{p_{f}}{p_{i}}\right)=R(300) \ln (2)=300 R \ln 2$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of work done by an ideal monoatomic gas to the heat supplied to it in an isobaric process is:
explanation:
Efficiency of heat engine is given by
$\eta=\dfrac{W}{Q}=\dfrac{P \Delta V}{n C_{P} \Delta T}=\dfrac{n R \Delta T}{n C_{P} \Delta T}=\dfrac{R}{C_{P}}=\dfrac{R}{\dfrac{5 R}{2}}=\dfrac{2}{5}$
For monoatomic gas $C_{P}=\dfrac{5}{2} R$.
subject: Physics

question:
The equation of state for a gas is given by
$P V=n R T+\alpha V$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is the number of moles and $\alpha$ is a positive constant. The initial temperature and pressure of one mole of the gas contained in a cylinder are $T_{0}$ and $P_{0}$ respectively. The work done by the gas when its temperature doubles isobarically will be:
explanation:
Given $PV = nRT + \alpha V\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\Rightarrow(P-\alpha) V=n R T$
$V=\dfrac{n R T}{(P-\alpha)}$
$d V=\dfrac{n R d T}{(P-\alpha)}$
Now work done is $\int d W=\int P d V$
From (i) $P d V=n R d T+\alpha d V$ ( $P=$ constant $)$
$\begin{aligned}& \int d W=\int n R d T+\alpha d V \\& \quad=\int n R d T+\dfrac{\int \alpha(n R d T)}{P-\alpha} \\& =n R \int_{T_{0}}^{2 T_{0}} d T+\dfrac{\alpha}{P_{0}-\alpha} n R \int_{T_{0}}^{2 T_{0}} d T\left(P=P_{0}=\text { constant }\right) \\& =n R T_{0}+\dfrac{\alpha}{P_{0}-\alpha} \cdot n R T_{0}=\dfrac{n R T_{0} P_{0}}{\left(P_{0}-\alpha\right)} \\& \therefore \quad W=\dfrac{R T_{0} P_{0}}{\left(P_{0}-\alpha\right)}(n=1)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal monoatomic gas in confined in a cylinder by a spring loaded piston of cross section $8.0 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^2}$. Initially the gas is at $300\,K$ and occupies a volume of $2.4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$ and spring is in its relaxed state as shown in figure. The gas is heated by a small heater until the piston moves out slowly by $0.1\,m$. The force constant of the spring is $8000\,N/m$ and the atmospheric pressure is $1.0 \times {10^5}\,N/{m^2}$. The cylinder and the piston are thermally insulated. The piston and the spring are massless and there is no friction between the piston and the cylinder. The final temperature of the gas will be : (Neglect the heat loss through the lead wires of the heater. The heat capacity of the heater coil is also negligible):-
explanation:
Area of the piston is $A = 8 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^2}$
${T_1} = 300\,K;{V_1} = 2.4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}\,$
${V_2} = {V_1} + A\Delta x$
$ = 2.4 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 8 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 0.1 = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}\,$
$K = 8000\,N/m;{T_2} = ?;{P_1} = {10^5}\,N/{m^2}$
Initially the piston is in equilibrium. Let ${P_0}$ is the atmospheric pressure.
${P_0} = {P_1}$ (pressure of the gas)
After expansion again the piston is in equilibrium
${P_2} = {P_0} + \frac{{Kx}}{A}$
${P_2} = {P_1} + \frac{{Kx}}{{\,A}} = {10^5} + \frac{{8000 \times 0.1}}{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2 \times {10^5}\,N/{m^2}$
$\frac{{{P_1}\,{V_1}}}{{\,{T_1}}} = \frac{{{P_2}\,{V_2}}}{{\,{T_2}}}$
$\frac{{{{10}^5} \times 2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{300}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^5} \times 3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{\,{T_2}}}\,$
${T_2} = \frac{{3.2 \times 300}}{{1.2}} = 800\,K$
subject: Physics

question: A gas is compressed from a volume of $2 m^{3}$ to a volume of $1\,{m^3}$ at a constant pressure of $100\,N/{m^2}$. Then it is heated at constant volume by supplying $150\,J$ of energy. As a result, the internal energy of the gas:
explanation:
From $1^{s t}$ law of thermodynamics
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+W \Rightarrow \Delta Q=\Delta U+P \Delta V$
$150=\Delta U+100(1-2)$
$\therefore \Delta U=150+100=250 J$
Thus the internal energy of the gas increases by $250\,J$
subject: Physics

question: During an adiabatic compression, $830\,J$ of work is done on $2{\rm{ }}moles$ of a diatomic ideal gas to reduced its volume by $50\,\% $. The change in its temperature is nearly : $\left( {R = 8.3\,J{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}} \right)$
explanation:
Work done $W = 830\,J$
No.of moles of gas, $n=2$
For diatomic gas $\gamma=1.4$
Work done during an adiabatic change
$W = \frac{{nR\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{{\gamma - 1}}$
$ \Rightarrow 830 = \frac{{2 \times 8.3(\Delta T)}}{{1.4 - 1}} = \frac{{2 \times 8.3(\Delta T)}}{{0.4}}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = \frac{{830 \times 0.4}}{{2 \times 8.3}} = 20\,K$
subject: Physics

question:
There are two identical chambers, completely thermally insulated from surroundings. Both chambers have a partition wall dividing the chambers in two compartments. Compartment 1 is filled with an ideal gas and compartment 3 is filled with a real gas. Compartments 2 and 4 are vacuum. A small hole (orifice) is made in the partition walls and the gases are allowed to expand in vacuum.
No change in the temperature of the gas takes place when ideal gas expands in vacuum. However, the temperature of real gas goes down (cooling) when it expands in vacuum.
The internal energy of an ideal gas is only kinetic. The internal energy of a real gas is kinetic as well as potential.
explanation: In ideal gases the molecules are considered as point particles and for point particles, there is no internal excitation, no vibration and no rotation. For an ideal gas the internal energy can only be translational kinetic energy and for real gas both kinetic as well as potential energy. The internal energy of an ideal gas is a function of temperature only so when ideal gas undergoes free expansion $W=0, \Delta Q=0$ (insulated chambers) $\Rightarrow \Delta U=0 \quad$ so temperature of the ideal gas remain constant. Whereas internal energy of a real gas is a function of both temperature and pressure. When a real gas undergoes free expansion the gas gets cooled.

options.text: Statement-1 is false and statement-2 is true.

Statement-1 and statement-2 both are true.
Statement- 2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true and statement-2 is false.

Statement-1 and statement-2 both are true.
Statement 2 is not correct explanation of Statement-1.

subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas at atmospheric pressure is adiabatically compressed so that its density becomes 32 times of its initial value. If the final pressure of gas is 128 atmospheres, the value of ' $\gamma$ ' of the gas is:
explanation:
Volume of the gas
$V=\dfrac{m}{d}$
$P V^{\gamma}=$ constant
$\dfrac{P^{\prime}}{P}=\left(\dfrac{V}{V^{\prime}}\right)^{\gamma}=\left(\dfrac{d^{\prime}}{d}\right)^{\gamma}$
$128=(32)^{\gamma}$
$\therefore \gamma=\dfrac{7}{5}=1.4$
subject: Physics

question: A container of volume $4{\rm{ }}{V_0}$ made of a perfectly non-conducting material is divided into two equal parts by a fixed rigid wall whose lower half is non-conducting and upper half is purely conducting. The right side of the wall is divided into equal parts (initially) by means of a massless non-conducting piston free to move as shown. Section $A$ contains $2\,mol$ of a gas while the section $B$ and $C$ contain $1\,mol$ each of the same gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ at pressure $P_{0}$. The heater in left part is switched on till the final pressure in section $C$ becomes $125 / 27 P_{0}$. Calculate: Final temperature in part $A$ :
explanation:
The final volume of the compartment $\mathrm{C}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0} V_{0}^{\gamma}=\dfrac{125 P_{0}}{27} V_{C}^{\gamma} \Rightarrow V_{C}^{\gamma}=\dfrac{27}{125} V_{0}^{\gamma} \\& V_{C}=\left(\dfrac{27}{125}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}} V_{0}=\left(\dfrac{27}{125}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}} V_{0} \Rightarrow V_{C}=\dfrac{9 V_{0}}{25}\end{aligned}$
The final volume of compartment $B$ is
$V_{B}=2 V_{0}-\dfrac{9 V_{0}}{25}=\dfrac{41 V_{0}}{25}$
The final temerature of compartment $\mathrm{B}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& T_{B}=\dfrac{P_{B} V_{B}}{n R} \\& P_{B}=P_{C}=\dfrac{125 P_{0}}{27} \text { (Mechanical equilibrium) } \\& V_{B}=\dfrac{41 V_{0}}{25}, n=1 \\& \Rightarrow \quad T_{B}=\dfrac{\dfrac{125 P_{0}}{27} \times \dfrac{41 V_{0}}{25}}{R}=\dfrac{205 P_{0} V_{0}}{27 R} \\& T_{A}=T_{B}=\dfrac{205 P_{0} V_{0}}{27 R}\end{aligned}$
Since the wall between $A$ & $B$ is conducting in nature.
subject: Physics

question: A thermally insulated rigid container contains an ideal gas heated by a filament of resistance $100\,\Omega $ through a current of $1\,A$ for $5\,\min $ then change in internal energy is
explanation:
Volume of the ideal gas is constant so
$W = P\Delta V = 0$
$\Delta Q = \Delta U \Rightarrow \Delta U = {i^2}Rt$
$ = {1^2} \times 100 \times 5 \times 60 = 30 \times {10^3} = 30\,kJ.$
subject: Physics

question: A horizontal frictionless piston, of negligible mass and heat capacity, divides a vertical insulated cylinder into two halves. Each half of the cylinder contains 1 mole of air at standard temperature and pressure $p_{o}$. A load of weight $W$ is now suspended from the piston, as shown in the figure. It pulls the piston down and comes to rest after a few oscillations. How large a volume does the compressed air in the lower part of the cylinder ultimately occupy if $W$ is very large?
explanation:
Let $A$ denote the cross-sectional area of the piston and $y$ be the vertical displacement between its initial and final equilibrium positions
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956244/preptracker/questions/q_38045_explanation_1764956244420.png
The decrease in potential energy of the weight is equal to increase in internal energy of the air inside the cylinder. Conservation of energy between the initial and the final states gives
$Wy = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {{p_1}A(h - y) + {p_2}A(h + y)} \right]$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, - \left[ {2{p_0}Ah} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $p_{1}$ is the final pressure in the lower part of the cylinder and $p_{2}$ that in the upper part.
The internal energy of a gas made up of diatomic molecules has been written in the form $\dfrac{5}{2} p V$.
As $W$ is very large the decrease in potential energy will be converted into internal energy.
The initial internal energy of the gas is negligible in comparsion with final energy.
$Wy = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {{p_1}A(h - y) + {p_2}A(h + y)} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
When the load is finally at rest,
$\left( {{p_1} - {p_2}} \right)A = W\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
The temperatures and the masses of the gases in the two halves are identical, and so their internal energies must be equal :
$\frac{5}{2}{p_1}A(h - y) = \frac{5}{2}{p_2}A(h + y)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
Equation (1), (2), (3) & (4) yield $y=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7}} h$ for the displacement of the piston, i.e., the gas in the lower part is compressed to 15 percent of its original volume.
subject: Physics

question: A cylindrical tube with adiabatic walls having volume $2 V_{o}$ contains an ideal monoatomic gas as shown in the figure. The tube is divided into two equal parts by a fixed super conducting wall. Initially the pressure and the temperature are $P_{o}, T_{o}$ on the left and $2 P_{o}, 2 T_{o}$ on the right. When system is left side for sufficient amount of time the temperature on both sides becomes equal. How much heat has flown from the gas on the right to the gas on the left?
explanation:
At thermal equilibrium the final temperatures on both sides are equal. Let $T$ is the final temperature. The pressures on both sides are also equal and $P$ is the final pressure on both sides.
The internal energy of a monoatomic gas is $U=n C_{v} T=n \dfrac{3}{2} R T$
By conserving the energy of the system initially and finally.
$\dfrac{3}{2} n R T_{0}+\dfrac{3}{2} n R\left(2 T_{0}\right)=\dfrac{3}{2} n R T+\dfrac{3}{2} n R T$
(No. of moles are same on both sides)
$\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{3 T_{0}}{2}$
The heat that has flown from right side to the left side is
$\Delta \mu=\dfrac{3}{2} n R T-\dfrac{3}{2} n R T_{0}=\dfrac{3}{4} n R T_{0}=\dfrac{3 P_{0} V_{0}}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: An adiabatic cylindrical tube is fitted with an adiabatic separator as shown in figure. Initially separator is in equilibrium and divides a tube in two equal parts. The separator can be slide into the tube by an external mechanism. An ideal gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ is injected in the two sides at equal pressures and temperatures. Now separator is slide to a position where it divides the tube in the ratio $7: 3$. Find the ratio of the temperatures in the two parts of the vessel.
explanation:
From adiabatic relation between temperature and volume
$T_{i} V_{i}^{(\gamma-1)}=T_{f} V_{f}^{(\gamma-1)}$
Let $V_{0}$ and $T_{0}$ are the volume of the gas and temperature initially in each part. After displacing the adiabatic separator the new volumes are $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ and temperatures are $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$
Given that $\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\dfrac{7}{3}$
$V_{1}+V_{2}=2 V_{0} \Rightarrow V_{1}=\dfrac{14 V_{0}}{10} \& V_{2}=\dfrac{6 V_{0}}{10}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_0}V_0^{\gamma - 1} = {T_1}{\left( {\frac{{14{V_0}}}{{10}}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${T_0}V_0^{\gamma - 1} = {T_2}{\left( {\frac{{6{V_0}}}{{10}}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) & (2) we get $\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{7}}$
subject: Physics

question: An insulated container has two chambers separated by a valve. Chamber I contains an ideal gas and the chamber II has vacuum. If the gas expands then predict the incorrect option
explanation: In free expansion $\Delta Q=0, \Delta T=0$, but it doesn't represent isothermal and adiabatic processes. Temperature remains constant and it does not represent isobaric process.

options.text: It does not represent isothermal process
It does not represent adiabatic process

subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas expand isothermally from a volume $V_{1}$ to $V_{2}$ and then compressed to original volume $V_{1}$ adiabatically. Initial pressure is $P_{1}$ and finally pressure is $P_{3}$. The total work done is $W$. Then
explanation:
From graph it is clear that $P_{3}>P_{1}$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956236/preptracker/questions/q_38029_explanation_1764956236010.png
Since area under adiabatic process $(BCED)$ is greater than that of isothermal process $(ABDE)$. Therefore net work done
$\begin{aligned}& W=W_{A B}+W_{B C} \\& W_{A B}=(+) v e ; W_{B C}=(-) v e \Rightarrow W=(-) v e\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A horizontal insulated cylindrical vessel of length $2 l$ is separated by a thin insulating piston into two equal parts as shown in fig. Each part contains $n$ moles of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$. The piston is connected to end faces of the cylinder with the help of two springs each of spring constant $k$. Initially both the springs are in relaxed state. The temperature of left part of cylinder is kept constant by some external means while heat is supplied to right part with the help of filament. If energy (heat) $Q$ is supplied to right part so that the piston moves to the left by $\dfrac{l}{4}$, then the change in internal energy of right part of cylinder is:
explanation:
As heat $Q$ supplied to right part, the piston moves towards left thus compressing the left spring and elongating right spring. To keep the temperature of left part constant some energy say $Q^{\prime}$ is transferred from gas to external arrangement (which is used to keep its temperature constant).
As temperature of left part is constant so change in internal energy of left part is zero.
Let $T_{f}$ is the final temperature of right part
$\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T=n C_{V}\left(T_{f}-T\right)$
Where $T_{f}$ is the final equilibrium temperature of right part.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956963/preptracker/questions/q_38017_explanation_1764956962723.png
Using ideal gas equation
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{p_{1} \times A \times \dfrac{3 l}{4}}{R T}=\dfrac{p_{0} A l}{R T} \quad \text { (Left part) } \\& \dfrac{p_{2} \times A \times \dfrac{5 l}{4}}{R T_{f}}=\dfrac{p_{0} A l}{R T} \quad \text { (Right part) }\end{aligned}$
From equilibrium of piston,
$\begin{gathered}p_{2}-p_{1}=\dfrac{k l}{4 A}+\dfrac{k l}{4 A}=\dfrac{k l}{2 A} \\P_{2}=P_{1}+\dfrac{k l}{2 A}=\dfrac{4 P_{0}}{3}+\dfrac{k l}{2 A} \\\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{0}}=\dfrac{4}{3}+\dfrac{k l}{2 A P_{0}} \\\Rightarrow T_{f}=\dfrac{5 T}{4}\left[\dfrac{k l^{2}}{2 n R T}+\dfrac{4}{3}\right] \text { where } p_{0}=\dfrac{n R T}{A l} \\T_{f}=\dfrac{5 k l^{2}}{8 n R}+\dfrac{5 T}{3} \\\Delta U=n \times \dfrac{3 R}{2} \times\left(T_{f}-T\right)=\dfrac{3 n R}{2}\left[\dfrac{5 k l^{2}}{8 n R}+\dfrac{2 T}{3}\right]\end{gathered}$

options.text: $\dfrac{3 n R}{2}\left[\dfrac{5 k l^{2}}{8 n R}+\dfrac{2 T}{3}\right]$

subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas at \[NTP\] is enclosed in an adiabatic vertical cylinder having an area of cross section \[A = 27\;c{m^2}\] between two light movable piston as shown in the fig. Spring with force constant \[k = 3700\;N/m\] is in a relaxed state initially. Now the lower piston is moved upwards a distance \(h / 2\), \(h\) being the initial length of gas column. It is observed that the upper piston moves up by a distance \(h / 16\). Final temperature of gas \[ = 4/3 \times 273\,K\]. Take \(\gamma\) for the gas to be \(3 / 2\). The value of \(h\) is:
explanation:
Final volume of gas
$V^{\prime}=A\left(h-\dfrac{h}{2}+\dfrac{h}{16}\right)=\dfrac{9 A h}{16}$
As the gas has to balance the additional spring force
Final pressure of the gas is
$P^{\prime}=p_{a}+\dfrac{K \cdot h / 16}{A}=\left(10^{5}+\dfrac{37 \times 10^{6} \times h}{16 \times 27}\right) N / m^{2}$
Now, $\dfrac{P^{\prime} V^{\prime}}{T^{\prime}}=\dfrac{P V}{T}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(10^{5}+\dfrac{37 \times 10^{6} \times h}{16 \times 27}\right) \times\left(\dfrac{9 \times A h}{16}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{4}{3} \times 273\right)}=\dfrac{10^{5} \times A h}{273}$
Solving, $h = 1.6\,m.$
subject: Physics

question: A resistance coil connected to an external battery is placed inside an adiabatic cylinder fitted with a frictionless piston of mass $m$ and containing an ideal gas. A current $i$ flows through the coil which has a resistance $R$. At what speed must the piston move upward in order that the temperature of the gas remains unchanged? Neglect atmospheric pressure.
explanation: The input electrical power and output mechanical powers must be same $m g \times v=i^{2} R$.
subject: Physics

question: A piston can freely move inside a horizontal cylinder closed from both ends. Initially, the piston separates the inside space of the cylinder into two equal parts each of volume $V_{0}$, in which an ideal gas is contained under the same pressure $P_{0}$ and at the same temperature. What work has to be performed in order to increase isothermally the volume of one part of gas $\eta$ times compared to that of the other by slowly moving the piston?
explanation:
In equilibrium position, $P_{1} A+F_{\text {agent }}=P_{2} A$ $F_{\text {agent }}=\left(P_{2}-P_{1}\right) A$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956934/preptracker/questions/q_38005_explanation_1764956934389.png
Elementary workdone by the agent
${F_{agent{\rm{ }}}}dx = \left( {{P_2} - {P_1}} \right)A \times dx = \left( {{P_2} - {P_1}} \right)dV\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Applying $\mathrm{PV}=$ constant for two parts, we have $P_{1}\left(V_{0}+A x\right)=P_{0} V_{0}$ and $P_{2}\left(V_{0}-A x\right)=P_{0} V_{0}$
$P_{1}=\dfrac{P_{0} V_{0}}{\left(V_{0}+A x\right)}$ and $P_{2}=\dfrac{P_{0} V_{0}}{\left(V_{0}-A x\right)}$
$\therefore P_{2}-P_{1}=\dfrac{P_{0} V_{0}(2 A x)}{V_{0}^{2}-A^{2} x^{2}}=\dfrac{2 P_{0} V_{0} V}{V_{0}^{2}-V^{2}} \quad(V=A x)$
When the volume of the left end is $\eta$ times the volume of right end, we have
$\left( {{V_0} + V} \right) = \eta \left( {{V_0} - V} \right)$
$V = \left( {\frac{{\eta - 1}}{{\eta + 1}}} \right){V_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
The workdone by the agent is given by
$\begin{aligned}& W=\int_{0}^{V}\left(P_{2}-P_{1}\right) d V=\int_{0}^{V} \dfrac{2 P_{0} V_{0} V}{V_{0}^{2}-V^{2}} d V \\& =-P_{0} V_{0}\left[\ln \left(V_{0}^{2}-V^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{V} \\& =-P_{0} V_{0}\left[\ln \left(V_{0}^{2}-V^{2}\right)-\ln V_{0}^{2}\right] \\& =-P_{0} V_{0}\left[\ln \left\{V_{0}^{2}-\left(\dfrac{\eta-1}{\eta+1}\right) V_{0}^{2}\right\}-\ln V_{0}^{2}\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In the given figure, an adiabatic cylindrical tube of $2 V_{o}$ is divided in two equal parts by a frictionless adiabatic separator. An ideal gas in left side of a tube having pressure $P_{1}$ and temperature $T_{1}$ where as in the right side having pressure $P_{2}$ and temperature $T_{2} . C_{p} / C_{v}=\gamma$ is the same for both the gases. The separator is slide slowly and is released at a position where it can stay in equilibrium. find the final volume of left part.
explanation:
An external agent displaces the system to the equilibrium position & we can't use conservation of energy for the system.
As the changes take place under adiabatic conditions.
${P_1}V_0^\gamma = PV_1^\gamma \quad \& \,\,\,{P_2}V_0^\gamma = PV_2^\gamma $
$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{V_1^\gamma }} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{{V_2^\gamma }} \Rightarrow {P_1}V_1^\gamma = {P_2}V_2^\gamma $
$P_1^{1/\gamma }{V_1} = P_2^{1/\gamma }{V_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
We know that ${V_1} + {V_2} = 2{V_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$V_{1}=\dfrac{2 V_{0} P_{1}^{1 / \gamma}}{P_{1}^{1 / \gamma}+P_{2}^{1 / \gamma}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two cylinders fitted with pistons and placed as shown, connected with string through a small tube of negligible volume, are filled with a gas at pressure $p_{o}$ and temperature $T_{o}$. The radius of smaller cylinder is half of the other. If the temperature is increased to $2 T_{o}$, find the pressure, if the piston of bigger cylinder moves towards left by 1 metre?
explanation:
When the bigger piston displaces towards left by $1\,m$ the smaller piston also displace by $1\,m$ towards left.
The length of the left side cylinder becomes $2\,m$ and right side cylinder becomes $0\,m$.
The initial volume ${V_o} = \pi {R^2}(1m) + \frac{{\pi {R^2}}}{4}(1\,m)$
The final volume $V=\pi R^{2}(2 m)$
As the number of moles are equal initially and finally.
$\begin{aligned}& n_{i}=n_{f} \\& \qquad \dfrac{P_{o} V_{o}}{T_{o}}=\dfrac{P V}{2 T_{o}} \Rightarrow P=\dfrac{5 P_{o}}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Find the pressure at which temperature attains its maximum value if the relation between pressure and volume for an ideal is $P=P_{0}+(1-\alpha) V^{2} ; \alpha>1$
explanation:
$P V=n R T$
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \quad P_{0}+\left(1-\alpha V^{2}\right) V=n R T \\\Rightarrow \quad T=\dfrac{\left[P_{0} V+(1-\alpha) V^{3}\right]}{n R} \\\dfrac{d T}{d V}=\dfrac{P_{0}}{n R}+\dfrac{3 V^{2}(1-\alpha)}{n R}=0 \\\Rightarrow \quad P_{0}=3(\alpha-1) V^{2} \\\Rightarrow \quad V^{2}=\dfrac{P_{3}}{3(\alpha-1)} \\\Rightarrow \quad P=P_{0}+(1-\alpha) \dfrac{P_{0}}{3(\alpha-1)}=P_{0}-\dfrac{P_{0}}{3}=\dfrac{2 P_{0}}{3}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: Consider a spherical shell of radius $R$ at temperature $T$. The black body radiation inside it can be considered as an ideal gas of photons with internal energy per unit volume $u=\dfrac{U}{V} \propto T^{4}$ and pressure $p=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{U}{V}\right)$. If the shell now undergoes an adiabatic expansion the relation between $T$ and $R$ is:
explanation:
As, $P=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{U}{V}\right)$
But $\dfrac{U}{V}=K T^{4} \quad(K$ is a constant $)$
So, $\quad P=\dfrac{1}{3} K T^{4}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n R T}{V}=\dfrac{1}{3} K T^{4} \quad[\text { As } P V=n R T]$

$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} T^{3}=\text { constant } \quad\left(V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right)$
Therefore, $T \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal gas at an initial temperature of $TK$ does $6R$ joules of work adiabatically. If the ratio of specific heat of this gas at constant pressure and at constant volume is $5 / 3$, the final temperature of gas will be
explanation:
In adiabatic process $\Delta Q=0$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta Q=\Delta U+W \\& W=-\Delta U=-n C_{V}\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right) \\& W=n C_{V}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) \\& 6 R=\dfrac{3}{2} R\left(T-T_{2}\right) \\& T_{2}=(T-4) K\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A mass of diatomic gas $(\gamma=1.4)$ at a pressure of $2\,atm$ is compressed adiabatically so that its temperature rise from $27^\circ C$ to $927^\circ C$. The pressure of the gas in final state is
explanation:
${T_1} = 273 + 27 = 300\,K$
${T_2} = 273 + 927 = 1200\,K$
Gas equation for adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned}& P V^{\gamma}=\text { constant } \\& p\left(\dfrac{T}{p}\right)^{\gamma}=\text { constant } \quad(\because P V=R T) \\& \dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} \text { or } p_{2}=p_{1}\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}\end{aligned}$
${p_2} = 2{\left( {\frac{{1200}}{{300}}} \right)^{\frac{{1.4}}{{1.4 - 1}}}}$
${p_2} = 256\,atm.$
subject: Physics

question: During an adiabatic process, the cube of the pressure is found to be inversely proportional to the fourth power of the volume. Then the ratio of specific heats is
explanation:
$P V^{\gamma}=$ constant for an adiabatic process
Given that
${P^3} \times {V^4} = $ constant
$\mathrm{PV}^{4 / 3}=$ constant.
$ \Rightarrow \quad g = \frac{4}{3} = 1.33$
subject: Physics

question: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its temperature. The ratio of $\dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}$ for the gas is
explanation:
Given adiabatic process $P \propto T^{3}$.
Ideal gas equation $ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{PV}}{{nR}}$
$P \propto {T^3}$
$P \propto {(PV)^3} \Rightarrow {P^2}\,{V^3} = P{V^{\frac{3}{2}}} = $ constant
Comparing with $P{V^\gamma } = $ constant
Hence, $\gamma=\dfrac{3}{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: A monoatomic gas is suddenly compressed to (1/81) of its initial volume adiabatically. The ratio of its final pressure to the initial pressure is (given the ratio of the specific heat of the given gas to be $5 / 3$ )
explanation:
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=(81)^{\frac{5}{3}}=\left(3^{3}\right)^{\frac{5}{3}} \\& =243\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The slopes of isothermal and adiabatic curves are related as
explanation:
$\dfrac{\text { Slope of adiabatic curve }}{\text { Slope of isothermal curve }}=\dfrac{(d P / d V)_{\text {adi }}}{(d P / d V)_{\text {iso }}}=+\gamma$
So slope to adiabatic curve is $\gamma\left(=\dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}\right)$ times of isothermal curve

options.text: Isothermal curve slope = adiabatic curve slope
Isothermal curve slope $=\gamma^{x}$ adiabatic curve slope
Adiabatic curve slope $=\gamma^{x}$ isothermal curve slope
Adiabatic curve slope $=\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ isothermal curve slope

subject: Physics

question: In an adiabatic expansion of an ideal gas the product of pressure and volume
explanation:
For an adiabatic process, $P V^{\gamma}=$ constant
${\rm{ or }}PV = \frac{{{\rm{ constant }}}}{{{V^{\gamma - 1}}}} \Rightarrow PV \propto \frac{1}{{{V^{\gamma - 1}}}}$
Since it is an adiabatic expansion, $V$ increases, therefore $PV$ decreases.

options.text: At first increases and then decreases

subject: Physics

question: During an isothermal expansion, a confined ideal gas does $ - 150\,J$ of work against its surroundings. This implies that
explanation:
From the first law of the thermodynamics
$\Delta U=Q-W$
For isothermaal processes, $\Delta U=0$
$\therefore Q=W$
Given, $W = 150\,J$ (Work done by the gas)
Where $Q$ is positive, the heat is added to the gas.

options.text: $300\,J$ of heat has been added to the gas
No heat is transferred because the process is isothermal
$150\,J$ of heat has been added to the gas
$150\,J$ heat has been removed from the gas

subject: Physics

question: In the equation $P V^{\gamma}=$ constant, the value of $\gamma$ is unity. Then the process is
explanation: $P V=$ constant represent isothermal process
subject: Physics

question: How much heat energy in joules must be supplied to $14\,g$ of nitrogen at room temperature to raise its temperature by $40^\circ C$ at constant pressure? (Mol. wt. of ${N_2} = 28{\rm{ }}g$)
explanation:
As nitrogen is diatomic, its molar specific heat at constant pressure is
$C_{p}=\dfrac{7}{2} R$
No. of moles, $n=\dfrac{14}{28}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \Delta Q=n C_{p} \Delta T$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{7}{2} R \times 40=70 R$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of ${O_2}$ gas is heated at constant pressure starting at $27^\circ C$. How much energy must be added to the gas as heat to double its volume?
explanation:
Here, ${V_1} = V,\,\,{V_2} = 2\,V$
${T_1} = 27^\circ C = 300\,K$
At constant pressure
$\frac{{{V_1}}}{{{T_1}}} = \frac{{{V_2}}}{{{T_2}}}\quad \therefore {T_2} = \left( {\frac{{{V_2}}}{{{V_1}}}} \right) \times {T_1} = 600\,K$
Heat absorbed by the gas at constant pressure
$ = n{C_p}\Delta T = 1 \times \frac{7}{2}R \times 300 = 1050R.$
subject: Physics

question: In thermodynamic processes which of the following statements is not true?
explanation: For an adiabatic process there should not be any exchange of heat between the system and its surroundings all walls of the container must be perfectly insulated. In adiabatic process gases obey $p V^{\gamma}=$ constant. In an isochoric process volume remains constant and for isobaric process pressure remains constant.

options.text: In an adiabatic processes the system is insulated from the surroundings
In an isochoric process pressure remains constant
In an isothermal process the temperature remains constant
In an adiabatic process $p V^{\gamma}=$ constant

subject: Physics

question: A cycle tyre bursts suddenly. What is the type of this process?
explanation: Sudden bursting of the cycle tyre is an adiabatic process.
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas system undergoes an isothermal process, then the work done during the process is
explanation:
Let an ideal gas goes isothermally from its initial state $\left(P_{1}, V_{1}\right)$ to the final state $\left(P_{2}, V_{2}\right)$, then work done
$W = \int\limits_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} P dV$. Taking $P V=n R T$ or $P=\dfrac{n R T}{V}$
Then, $W = \int\limits_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} {\frac{{nRT}}{V}} dV = nRT\int\limits_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} {\frac{{dV}}{V}} = nRT\ln \frac{{{V_2}}}{{{V_1}}}$
subject: Physics

question: What amount of heat must be supplied to $35\,g$ of oxygen at room temperature to raise its temperature. by $80^\circ C$ at constant volume (molecular mass of oxygen is 32 and $R = 8.3\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{k^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
Here mass of oxygen $(\mathrm{m})=35 \mathrm{~g}$, molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{M})=32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ rise in temperature, $\Delta T = 80^\circ C$
$\therefore$ number of moles $n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{{35}}{{32}} = 1.09\,mol$
As oxygen is a diatomic gas, then molar specific heat at constant volume is $C_{v}=\dfrac{5}{2} R$ and amount of heat supplied to gas $Q = n{C_v}\Delta T$
$ = 1.09 \times \frac{5}{2}R \times 80 = 1.09 \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.3 \times 80$
$ = 1.81\,kJ$
subject: Physics

question: $310\,J$ of heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 moles of an ideal gas at constant pressure from $25^\circ C$ to $35^\circ C$. The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of the gas through the same range at constant volume is
explanation:
At constant pressure, $\Delta Q = n{C_p}\Delta T$
$ \Rightarrow 310 = 2 \times {C_P} \times (35 - 25) = 20{C_P}$ or ${C_P} = \frac{{310}}{{20}}$
At constant volume, heat required $ = n{C_V}\Delta T$
$\Delta Q = 2 \times \left( {{C_P} - R} \right) \times (35 - 25)$
$\Delta Q = 2 \times (15.5 - 8.3) \times 10$
$\Delta Q = 2 \times 7.2 \times 10 = 144\,\,J$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal gas undergoes an isothermal change at temperature $T$ so that its volume 7 times its initial value. $R$ is the molar gas constant. Work done by the gas during this change is
explanation:
Work done by the gas during the isothermal process.
$W=n R T \ln \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$
$=(1) R T \ln \left(\dfrac{7 V}{V}\right)=R T \ln 7$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas is compressed to $\dfrac{1}{4} t h$ its initial volume by means of several process. Which of the process results in the maximum work done on the gas?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955900/preptracker/questions/q_37792_explanation_1764955899606.png
Work done $=$ Negative of area under $P - V$ curve So, largest work takes place in adiabatic process.
subject: Physics

question: If $\Delta U$ and $-\Delta W$ represent the increase in internal energy and work done by the system respectively in a thermodynamic process, which of the following is true?
explanation:
From the first law of thermodynamics
$Q=\Delta U+W$
For adiabatic process $Q = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta U = - W$

options.text: $\Delta U=-\Delta W$, in an adiabatic process
$\Delta U=\Delta W$, in an isothermal process
$\Delta U=\Delta W$, in an adiabatic process
$\Delta U=-\Delta W$, is an isothermal process

subject: Physics

question: In which of the processes, does the internal energy of the system remain constant?
explanation: The internal energy of a system remains a constant when the temperature does not change.
subject: Physics

question: Two cylinders $A$ and $B$ of equal capacity are connected to each other via a stop cock. A contains an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure. $B$ is completely evacuated. The entire system is thermally insulated. The stop cock is suddenly opened. The process is:
explanation: $\Delta U=0$. Hence $U_{i}=U_{f} \Rightarrow \Delta T=0$
subject: Physics

question: In which of the following processes, heat is neither absorbed nor released by a system?
explanation: In adiabatic process, heat exchange does not take place .
subject: Physics

question: The volume \[(V)\] of a monatomic gas varies with its temperature \[(T)\], as shown in the graph. The ratio of work done by the gas, to the heat absorbed by it, when it undergoes a change from state \[A\] to state \[B\], is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955864/preptracker/questions/q_37764_explanation_1764955864206.png
The given process is isobaric.
$\begin{aligned}& W=P \Delta V=n R \Delta T \\& \Delta U=\dfrac{f}{2} n R \Delta T \\& \Delta Q=\Delta U+W=\left(\dfrac{f}{2}+1\right) n R \Delta T\end{aligned}$
Ratio $\dfrac{W}{\Delta Q}=\dfrac{1}{\dfrac{f}{2}+1}=\dfrac{2}{f+2}=\dfrac{2}{5}$
For monoatomic gas $f=3$
subject: Physics

question: A gas is compressed isothermally to half its initial volume. The same gas is compressed separately through an adiabatic process untill its volume is again reduced to half. Then,
explanation:
${W_{ext}} = $ negative of area with volume-axis
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955856/preptracker/questions/q_37753_explanation_1764955856514.png
$W$ ( adiabatic ) > $W$ ( isothermal )

options.text: Compressing the gas isothermally will require more work to be done.
Compressing the gas through adiabatic process will require more work to be done.
Compressing the gas isothermally or adiabatically will require the same amount of work.
Which of the case(whether compressing through isothermal or through adiabatic process) requires more work will depend upon the atomicity of the gas.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In adiabatic process, work is independent of the path.


Reason: In adiabatic process, work done is equal to negative of change in internal energy.
explanation:
In adiabatic process, heat transfer to the thermodynamic system is zero, i.e., $\Delta Q=0$.
Hence, by first law of thermodynamics,
$\begin{gathered}\Delta Q=0=\Delta U+\Delta W \\\therefore \quad W=-\Delta U\end{gathered}$
So, work done is equal to negative of change in internal energy.
Since, the internal energy is a function of thermodynamic state of the system.
It does not depend on the path, therefore work done is also independent of path in adiabatic process.
Therefore, Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Change of state is an example of isothermal process.


Reason: Change of state from solid to liquid occurs only at melting point of solid and change of state from liquid to gas occurs only at boiling point of liquid. thus, there is no change of temperature during change of state.
explanation: Option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The adiabatic curves intersect each other at a certain point.


Reason: Rapid changes of gases can be treated as adiabatic changes
explanation: Adiabatic curves never intersect each other as they are always parallel. Rapid processes are always adiabatic as there is no time for exchange of heat. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In cyclic process, $\Delta U=0$.


Reason: Work done in a cyclic path is always zero.
explanation: In cyclic process, the system returns to its initial state so $\Delta U=0$ work is a path function so $W_{\text {cycle }}$ need not to be zero.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Rotating blade in a liquid stops after a certain time is a irreversible process.


Reason: Irreversibility arises as the system attains a non-equilibrium state.
explanation:
Irreversibility mainly arises due to dissipative factor like friction, viscosity, etc and as many processes takes the system to non-equilibrium state.
So, when the blade is rotated in a liquid, then due to the viscosity, the system becomes irreversible as it stops after a certain time. Thus, it is a irreversible process. Irreversibility arises as the system attains equilibrium state. Assertion is correct reason is false. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
The isothermal curves intersect each other at a certain point.
The isothermal changes takes place slowly, so the isothermal curves have very little slope.
explanation: Isothermal curves never intersect each other. Isothermal changes take place slowly, as they have a very little slope. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
The temperature of a gas does not change, when it undergoes an adiabatic process.
During adiabatic process, heat energy is exchanged between a system and surroundings.
explanation: Temperature decreases during adiabatic expansion while it increases during adiabatic compression. Heat change is zero in adiabatic process. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
In an adiabatic process, change in internal energy is equal to magnitude of work done by the gas in the process.
The temperature remains constant in an adiabatic process.
explanation:
In an adiabatic process $d Q=0$.
$\therefore \quad d U=-d W \quad(\therefore d Q=d U+d W)$
In an adiabatic process, temperature either rises or falls. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Thermodynamic processes in nature are irreversible.
Dissipative effects cannot be eliminated.
explanation: The thermodynamic process is irreversible as there always occurs a loss of energy due to energy spent in working against the dissipative force, which is not recovered back. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
In an isothermal expansion, the gas absorbs heat and does work while in an isothermal compression, work is done on the gas by the environment and heat is released.
In an isothermal process, there is no change in internal energy of an ideal gas.
explanation: From first law of thermodynamics, in isothermal expansion heat supplied to the gas equals the work done by the gas. In isothermal process temperature is constant. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
For an isothermal process,
I. Pressure of a given mass of a gas varies inversely as its volume.
II. During expansion of gas, work is done by the gas on environment.
III. During compression heat is released
Choose the correct statement
explanation:
For an isothermal process, temperature is fixed, so
$p V=\text { constant } \Rightarrow P \propto \dfrac{1}{V}$
In an isothermal expansion, gas absorbs heat and does work on the environment.
In an isothermal compression, work is done on the gas by the environment and heat is released.
So, option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: Compressed air in the tube of a wheel of a cycle at normal temperature suddenly starts coming out from a puncture. The air inside
explanation: This is an adiabatic expansion. So temperature falls.

options.text: May become hotter or cooler depending upon the amount of water vapour present

subject: Physics

question: Consider two containers $A$ and $B$ containing identical gases at the same pressure, volume and temperature. The gas in container  $A$ is compressed to half of its original volume isothermally while the gas in container $B$ is compressed to half of its original value adiabatically. The ratio of final pressure of gas in $B$ to that of gas in $A$ is
explanation:
When the compression is isothermal for gas in $A$,
$p_{2} V_{2}=p_{1} V_{1}$
$p_{2}=\dfrac{p_{1} V_{1}}{V_{2}}=p_{1} \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{1} / 2}=2 p_{1}$
For gas in $B$, when compression is adiabatic,
$\begin{aligned}& p_{2}{ }^{\prime} V_{2}^{\gamma}{ }^{\prime}=p_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma} \\& p_{2}{ }^{\prime}=p_{1}\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}{ }^{\prime}}\right)=p_{1}\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{1} / 2}\right)^{\gamma}=2^{\gamma} p_{1} \\& \therefore \dfrac{p_{2}{ }^{\prime}}{p_{2}}=\dfrac{2^{\gamma} p_{1}}{2 p_{1}}=2^{\gamma-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A cylinder with a movable piston contains 3 moles of hydrogen at standard temperature and pressure. The walls of the cylinder are made of heat insulator, and the piston is insulated by having a pile of sand on it By what factor does the pressure of the gas increase, if the gas is compressed to half its original volume?
explanation:
Let initial volume of the gas in the cylinder be $V$.
$\therefore \quad V_{1}=V$
and $V_{2}=V / 2$
As hydrogen is a diatomic gas
$\therefore \quad \gamma=\dfrac{7}{5}=1.4$
For an adiabatic change, $p_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=p_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
$\therefore \dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=(2)^{1.4}=2.64$
subject: Physics

question: For a process of monoatomic gas pressure $(P)$ and volume $(V)$ related as $P V^{-3}=$ constant. What will be the molar heat capacity of gas ?
explanation:
The polytropic equation is $P V^{x}=$ constant
$x=-3$
$P V^{-3}=$ constant
$C=\dfrac{R}{\gamma-1}-\dfrac{R}{x-1} \gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}$
$C=\dfrac{R}{\dfrac{5}{3}-1}-\dfrac{R}{-3-1}=\dfrac{3 R}{2}+\dfrac{R}{4}=\dfrac{7}{4} R$
subject: Physics

question: A gas expand with temperature according to the relation $V=K T^{2 / 3}$. What is the work done when the temperature changes by $30^\circ C$ ?
explanation:
$W=\int P d V=\int \dfrac{R T}{V} d V$
Since $V=K T^{2 / 3}$
Hence, $d V=\dfrac{2}{3} K T^{-1 / 3} d T \quad \therefore \dfrac{d V}{V}=\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{d T}{T}$
Hence
$W=\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{R T}{T} d T=\dfrac{2}{3} R\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)=\dfrac{2}{3} R(30)=20 R$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas is taken through a quasi-static process described by $P=\alpha V^{2}$, with $\alpha  = 5.00\,atm/{m^6}$. The gas is expanded to twice its original volume of  $1.00\,{m^3}$. How much work is done by the gas in expanding gas in this process?
explanation:
The work done by the gas $W=\int_{i}^{f} P d V$
$W=\int_{i}^{f} \alpha V^{2} d V=\dfrac{1}{3} \alpha\left(V_{f}^{3}-V_{i}^{3}\right)$
${V_f} = 2{V_i} = 2\left( {1.00\,{m^3}} \right) = 2.00\,{m^3}$
$\left. {W = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {5.00\,atm/{m^6}} \right)\left( {1.013 \times {{10}^5}\,Pa/atm} \right)} \right]$
$ \times \left[ {{{\left( {2.00{m^3}} \right)}^3} - {{\left( {1.00{m^3}} \right)}^3}} \right] = 1.18\,MJ$
subject: Physics

question: A monoatomic gas is enclosed in a cylinder with a movable frictionless piston. Its initial thermodynamic state at pressure ${P_i} = {10^5}\,Pa$ and volume ${V_i} = {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$ changes to a final state at ${P_f} = (1/32) \times {10^5}\,Pa$ and ${V_f} = 8 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$ in an adiabatic quasi-static process, The amount of heat supplied to the system in the process is
explanation:
$\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3} \Rightarrow$ monoatomic gas.
From first law of thermodynamics
$\Delta Q = W + \Delta U$
$W = {P_i}\Delta V = {10^5} \times 7 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 700\,\,J$
$\Delta U = n{C_V}\Delta T = \frac{3}{2}\left[ {{P_f}{V_f} - {P_i}{V_i}} \right] = - \frac{{900}}{8}\,\,J.$
So, $\Delta Q = W + \Delta U = 588\,\,J$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas at $27^\circ C$ is compressed adiabatically to $\dfrac{8}{27}$ of its original volume. If $\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}$, then rise in temperature is
subject: Physics

question: In an adiabatic change, the pressure and temperature of a monoatomic gas are related with relation as $P \propto T^{C}$, where $C$ is equal to:
explanation:
For adiabatic process,
$P V^{\gamma}=$ constant$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Ideal gas relation is
$ \Rightarrow V = \frac{{nRT}}{P}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$P\left(\dfrac{R T}{P}\right)^{\gamma}=$ constant $\Rightarrow \dfrac{T^{\gamma}}{P^{\gamma-1}}=$ constant
Given, $P \propto T^{C}$
$\Rightarrow P \alpha T^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} \Rightarrow C=\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}$
For a monoatomic gas $\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3} \Rightarrow C=\dfrac{5}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A monoatomic ideal gas, initially at temperature \(T_{1}\), is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a frictionless piston. The gas is allowed to expand adiabatically to a temperature \(T_{2}\) by releasing the piston suddenly. If \(L_{1}\) and \(L_{2}\) are the lengths of the gas column before and after expansion respectively, then \(T_{1} / T_{2}\) is given by
explanation:
$T_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma-1}=T_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma-1}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{\gamma-1}=\left(\dfrac{L_{2} A}{L_{1} A}\right)^{\frac{5}{3}-1}=\left(\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}$.
subject: Physics

question: $1\,g$ mole of an ideal gas at STP is subjected to a reversible adiabatic expansion to double its volume. Find the change in internal energy $(\gamma=1.4)$.
subject: Physics

question: A tyre pumped to a pressure $3.375 \mathrm{~atm}$ at $27^\circ C$ suddenly bursts. What is the final temperature $(\gamma=1.5)$ ?
explanation:
Sudden bursting of a tyre can be treated as adiabatic expansion
$T_{1}^{\gamma} P_{1}^{1-\gamma}=T_{2}^{\gamma} P_{2}^{1-\gamma}$
$\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=\left(\dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}\right)^{\gamma-1} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{300}{T_{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(\dfrac{3.375}{1}\right)^{3 / 2-1}$
${T_2} = \frac{{300}}{{{{(3.375)}^{1/3}}}} = 200\,K = - 73^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of ${O_2}$ gas having a volume equal to $22.4\,liters$ at $0^\circ C$ and 1 atomspheric pressure is compressed isothermally so that its volume reduces to $11.2\,liters$. The work done in this process is
explanation:
$W=-n R T \log _{e} \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$
$=-1 \times 8.31 \times(273+0) \log _{e}\left(\dfrac{22.4}{11.2}\right)$
$ = - 831 \times 273 \times {\log _e}2 = - 1572.5\,\,J$
$\left[\because \log _{e} 2=0.693\right]$
subject: Physics

question: A vessel containing 5 liters of a gas at $0.8\,m$ pressure is connected to an evacuated vessel of volume 3 litres. The resultant pressure inside will be (assuming temperature is constant)
explanation:
Temperature remains constant
$P_{1} V_{1}=P_{2} V_{2}$
$0.8 \times 5 = {P_2} \times (3 + 5) \Rightarrow {P_2} = 0.5\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A frictionless piston-cylinder based enclosure contains some amount of gas at a pressure of $400\,k\,Pa$. Then heat is transferred to the gas at constant pressure in a quasi-static process. The piston moves up slowly through a height of $10\,cm$. If the piston has a cross-section area of $0.3\,{m^2}$, the work done by the gas in this process is
explanation:
$W=P \times \Delta V=400 \times 10^{3} \times 0.3 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}$
$ = 400 \times 10 \times 3 = 12\,kJ.$
subject: Physics

question: Two $kg$ of water is converted into steam by boiling at atmospheric pressure. The volume changes from $2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$ to $3.34\,{m^3}$. The work done by the system is about
explanation:
$W=P \Delta V=1.01 \times 10^{5}\left(3.34-2 \times 10^{-3}\right)$
$ = 337 \times {10^3}\,J \approx 340\,kJ$
subject: Physics

question: A vessel contains an ideal monoatomic gas which expands at constant pressure, when heat $Q$ is given to it. Then the work done in expansion is
explanation:
For process at constant pressure
$Q=n C_{P} \Delta T=\dfrac{5}{2} n R \Delta T$ and
$W=P \Delta V=n R \Delta T=\dfrac{2}{5} Q$
subject: Physics

question: In an isochoric process, the correct ratio is
explanation: $\dfrac{\Delta Q}{W}=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta Q-\Delta U}=\dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{P}-C_{V}}=\dfrac{\gamma}{(\gamma-1)}$
subject: Physics

question: If $R = $ universal gas constant, the amount of heat needed to rise the temperature of $2\,\,mol$ of an ideal monoatomic gas from $273\,K$ to $373\,K$ when no work is done is
explanation: $\Delta Q=\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T=2 \times \dfrac{3 R}{2} \times 100=300 R$
subject: Physics

question: A thermally insulated vessel contains an ideal gas of molecular mass $\mathrm{M}$ and ratio of specific heat $\gamma$. It is moving with speed $v$ and it suddenly brought to rest. Assuming no heat is lost to the surroundings, its temperature increases by
explanation:
As on heat is lost, therefore
Loss of kinetic energy $=$ Gain of internal energy of gas
i.e., $\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=n C_{V} \Delta T \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{m}{M} \cdot \dfrac{R}{\gamma-1} \Delta T$
$\Rightarrow \Delta T=\dfrac{M v^{2}(\gamma-1)}{2 R}$
subject: Physics

question:
In the free expansion of a gas in an adiabatic vessel
(i) $W=0$ (ii) $\Delta Q=0$ (iii) $\Delta U=0$
explanation: In the free expansion $W=0, \Delta Q=0$ (adiabatic vessel) then $\Delta U=0$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas mixture filled inside a balloon expands according to the relation $P V^{2 / 3}=$ constant the temperature inside the balloon is
explanation:
Given that
$P V^{2 / 3}=\text { constant }$
From ideal gas equation
$PV = nRT$
$ \Rightarrow T{V^{ - 1/3}} = {\rm{ }}const{\rm{ }}$
$T\alpha {V^{1/3}}$
As $V$ increases $T$ also increases.
subject: Physics

question: Pressure $P$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ of a certain material are related by $P = \alpha {T^2}/V$, where $\alpha$ is constant. Work done by the material when temperature changes from $T_{0}$ and $2 T_{0}$ and pressure remains constant is
explanation:
The given relation is $P = \frac{{\alpha {T^2}}}{V}$
$\therefore \quad V = \frac{{\alpha {T^2}}}{P}$
As pressure is kept constant,
$\therefore \quad dV = \left( {\frac{{2\alpha T}}{P}} \right)dT$
Now, $W = \int P dV = \int_{{T_0}}^{2{T_0}} P \left( {\frac{{2\alpha T}}{P}} \right)dT$
$\,\,\,\,\, = 2\alpha \left[ {\frac{{{T^2}}}{2}} \right]_{{T_0}}^{2{T_0}} = 3\alpha T_0^2$
subject: Physics

question: A monoatomic gas at a pressure $P$, having a volume $V$ expands isothermally to a volume $2\,V$ and then adiabatically to a volume $16\,V$. The final pressure of the gas is : ( take $\left.\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}\right)$
explanation:
For isothermal process
$\dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}=2$
For adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{3}}=\left(\dfrac{V_{3}}{V_{2}}\right)^{5 / 3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{3}}=\left(\dfrac{16 V}{2 V}\right)^{5 / 3}=2^{5}=32 \\& \Rightarrow P_{3}=\dfrac{P_{2}}{32} \Rightarrow P_{2}=\dfrac{P}{64}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylindrical container supports a freely moving piston of mass $M$. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross-sectional area $A$. When the piston is in equilibrium the volume of the gas is $V_{0}$ and its pressure is $P_{0}$. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency.
explanation:
When piston is slightly displaced through equilibrium position, it will oscillate under a net restoring force. The piston will perform SHM.
Pressure applied by piston
$\dfrac{M g}{A}=P_{0}$ (At equilibrium)
$M g=P_{0} A$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955505/preptracker/questions/q_37435_explanation_1764955505177.png
Let piston be moved by a distance $x$
Here, the system is completely isolated so it is adiabatic.
$\begin{aligned}& p_{0} V_{0}^{\gamma}=p V^{\gamma} \\& p_{0} A x_{0}^{\gamma}=p A^{\gamma}\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma} \\& p=\dfrac{p_{0} x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\end{aligned}$
The equation of motion for the piston
$Mg - \left( {\frac{{{p_0}x_0^\gamma }}{{{{\left( {{x_0} - x} \right)}^\gamma }}}} \right)A = {F_{restoring{\rm{ }}}}$
${p_0}A\left( {1 - \frac{{x_0^\gamma }}{{{{\left( {{x_0} - x} \right)}^\gamma }}}} \right) = {F_{restoring{\rm{ }}}}$
${p_0}A\left[ {1 - \frac{1}{{\left( {1 - \frac{x}{{{x_0}}}} \right)}}\gamma } \right] = {F_{restoring{\rm{ }}}}$
${p_0}A\left( {1 - {{\left( {1 - \frac{x}{{{x_0}}}} \right)}^{ - \gamma }}} \right) = {F_{restoring{\rm{ }}}}$
$F = Ma = - \frac{{\gamma {p_0}Ax}}{{{x_0}}}\left( {{{\left( {1 - \frac{x}{{{x_0}}}} \right)}^{ - \gamma }} \approx 1 + \frac{{\gamma x}}{{{x_0}}}} \right)$
$a = - \left( {\frac{{\gamma {p_0}A}}{{M{x_0}}}} \right)x = - {\omega ^2}x = - 4{\pi ^2}{v^2}x$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{\gamma {p_0}A}}{{{x_0}M}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{\gamma {p_0}{A^2}}}{{M{V_0}}}} $
$$\left[ {\because {x_0} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{A}} \right]$$
subject: Physics

question: During an adiabatic process, the density of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of temperature. The degree of freedom of gas molecule is
explanation:
For adiabatic process, $T{V^{\gamma - 1}} = $ constant
$T{\left( {\frac{m}{\rho }} \right)^{\gamma - 1}} = $ constant
$\dfrac{T}{\rho^{\gamma-1}}=$ constant
$\rho \propto T^{1 / \gamma-1} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\gamma-1}=3 \Rightarrow \gamma=4 / 3$
$f=\dfrac{2}{\gamma-1}=\dfrac{2}{\left(\dfrac{4}{3}-1\right)}=6$
subject: Physics

question: A sound wave passing through air at NTP produces a pressure of  $0.001\,dyne/c{m^2}$ during a compression. The corresponding change in temperature (given $\gamma=1.5$ and assume gas to be ideal) is
explanation:
We generally assume that a sound wave propagates under adiabatic condition.
$T^{\gamma} \cdot P^{(1-\gamma)}=$ const.
Differentiating
$\gamma \cdot T^{\gamma-1} \cdot d T \cdot P^{(1-\gamma)}+T^{\gamma} \cdot(1-\gamma) P^{-\gamma} \cdot d P=0$
$\Rightarrow d T=\dfrac{(\gamma-1) T}{\gamma P} d P$
$ \Rightarrow dT = \left( {\frac{{1.5 - 1}}{{1.5}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{273}}{{{{10}^5}\left( {\,N/{m^2}} \right)}} \times 0.001 \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 4}}}}\frac{N}{{{m^2}}}} \right)$
$ = 8.97 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,K$
subject: Physics

question: If $\gamma$ denotes the ratio of two specific heats of gas, the ratio of slope of adiabatic and isothermal $\mathrm{PV}$ curves at their point of intersection is
explanation: Slope of adiabatic curve $=\gamma \times$ (Slope of isothermal curve)
subject: Physics

question: When an ideal gas in a cylinder was compressed isothermally by a piston, the work done on the gas was found to be $1.5 \times 10^{4}$ joules. During this process
explanation:
In isothermal compression, there is always an increase of heat which must flow out of the gas.
$$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W \Rightarrow \Delta Q = \Delta W\quad [\because \Delta U = 0]$$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta Q = - 1.5 \times {10^4}\,J = \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^4}}}{{4.18}}cal = - 3.6 \times {10^3}cal.$

options.text: $3.6 \times {10^3}cal$ of heat flowed into the gas
$3.6 \times {10^3}cal$ of heat flowed goes out from the gas
$1.5 \times {10^4}cal$ of heat flowed goes out from the gas
$1.5 \times {10^4}cal$ of heat flowed into the gas

subject: Physics

question: A container having 1 mole of a gas at a temperature $27^\circ C$ has a movable piston which maintains at constant pressure in container of 1 atm. The gas is compressed until temperature becomes $127^\circ C$. The work done is $\left( {{C_p}} \right.$ for gas is $\left. {7.03{\mkern 1mu} \,cal/mol{\mkern 1mu} K} \right)$
explanation:
At constant pressure
$\Delta W = P\Delta V = nR\Delta T = 1 \times 8.31 \times 100 = 831\,J$
subject: Physics

question: When 2 moles of air is given 70 calorie of heat, its temperature changes from $20^\circ C$ to $25^\circ C$ at constant pressure. The amount of heat required to rise the temperature of air through same range $\left( {20^\circ C} \right.$ to $\left. {25^\circ C} \right)$ at constant volume is $\left(\gamma=\dfrac{7}{5}\right)$
explanation:
$\Delta Q_{P}=n C_{P} \Delta T$
$\Delta Q_{V}=n C_{V} \Delta T$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta {Q_P}}}{{\Delta {Q_V}}} = \gamma \Rightarrow \Delta {Q_V} = 50\,cal$
subject: Physics

question: In an isobaric process, the correct ratio is
explanation: $\Delta Q: \Delta U=n C_{P} \Delta T: n C_{V} \Delta T=\dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}=\gamma: 1$
subject: Physics

question: A diatomic gas is heated at constant pressure. What fraction of the heat energy is used to increase the internal energy?
explanation:
$\Delta Q=n C_{P} \Delta T$
$\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T$
$\dfrac{\Delta U}{\Delta Q}=\dfrac{C_{V}}{C_{P}}=\dfrac{1}{\gamma}=\dfrac{5}{7}$
subject: Physics

question: In a cyclic process, work done by the system is
explanation: For cyclic forces $\Delta U=0$ So, $\Delta Q=\Delta W$

options.text: Equal to the heat change across the system
Independent of heat given to the system
More than the heat given to system

subject: Physics

question: The volume of gas is reduced adiabatically to (1/4) of its volume at $27^\circ C$. If $\gamma=1.4$ the new temperature will be:
explanation:
For adiabatic change the relation between temperature and volume is $T V^{\gamma-1}=$ constant where $\gamma$ is ratio of specific heats of the gas.
Given, $T_{1}=27+273=300 K, V_{1}=V, V_{2}=\dfrac{V}{4}$
$T_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma-1}=T_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma-1} \Rightarrow T_{2}=\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma-1} \times T_{1}$
$T_{2}=\left(\dfrac{V}{V / 4}\right)^{1.4-1} \times 300$
${T_2} = {(4)^{0.4}} \times 300 = 300 \times {(4)^{0.4}}\,K$
subject: Physics

question: In adiabatic process, $R=\dfrac{2}{3} C_{V}$. The pressure of the gas will be proportional to:
explanation:
$R=\dfrac{2}{3} C_{V}$
We know, $C_{p}-C_{V}=R$
$\gamma-1=\dfrac{R}{C_{V}} \Rightarrow R=C_{V}(\gamma-1)$
Comparing $\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}$
$P^{1-\gamma} T^{\gamma}=$ constant
$P \propto T^{(\gamma / \gamma-1)}$, Given $\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}$
$\dfrac{\gamma}{(\gamma-1)}=\dfrac{5}{2}$
So, $P \propto T^{5 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: The adiabatic Bulk modulus of a perfect gas at a pressure is given by
explanation:
For adiabatic process $P V^{\gamma}=$ constant
$\begin{aligned}& P \gamma V^{\gamma-1} d V+V^{\gamma} d P=0 \\& \dfrac{P \gamma}{V} d V+d P=0\end{aligned}$
Bulk modulus $=\dfrac{-d P}{d V / V}=\gamma P$
subject: Physics

question: Temperature of the system decreases in the process of
subject: Physics

question: $\Delta U+\Delta W=0$ is valid for
explanation:
In adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta Q=0 \Rightarrow \Delta U+\Delta W=0 \\& (\because \Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In case of an adiabatic process the correct relation in terms of pressure $p$ and density $\rho$ of a gas is:-
explanation:
For adiabatic process $p V^{\gamma}=$ constant
$\begin{aligned}& p\left(\dfrac{M}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma}=\text { constant } \\& p \cdot M^{\gamma} \cdot \rho^{-\gamma}=\text { constant } \\& \text { p. } \rho^{-\gamma}=\text { constant }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In adiabatic expansion of a gas
explanation: In adiabatic expansion as volume increases pressure and temperature decreases. In adiabatic expansion work done by the gas is at the expense of its own internal energy so temperature decreases.

question: In which of the process the internal kinetic energy of the system varies ?

subject: Physics

question: The specific heat of a gas in an isothermal process is
explanation: In isothermal process temperature remains constant. i.e., $\Delta T=0$. Hence according to $C=\dfrac{Q}{m \Delta T} \Rightarrow C_{i s o}=\infty$
subject: Physics

question: Certain amount of heat supplied to an ideal gas under isothermal conditions will result in
explanation:
At constant temp $d U=0$
$\therefore \quad d Q=d W$

options.text: Doing external work and a change in temperature
An increase in the internal energy of the gas

subject: Physics

question: Equation of state in an isothermal process is
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of work done by an ideal diatomic gas to the heat supplied by the gas in an isobaric process is
explanation:
$\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T=n(5 / 2) R \Delta T$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta Q=n C_{P} \Delta T=n(7 / 2) R \Delta T \\& W=\Delta Q-\Delta U=\dfrac{n 7}{2} R \Delta T-\dfrac{n 5}{2} R \Delta T=n R \Delta T \\& \dfrac{W}{\Delta U}=\dfrac{2}{7}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas is heated at a constant pressure of one atmosphere from $0^\circ C$ to $100^\circ C$. Then the change in the internal energy is
explanation:
Change in internal energy is $\Delta U=n C_{V} \Delta T$
For monoatomic gas $C_{V}=\dfrac{3}{2} R$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta U=n \dfrac{3}{2} R \Delta T=1 \times \dfrac{3}{2} \times 8.31 \times(100-0) \\& =12.48 \times 10^{2} J .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: When heat is given to a gas in an isobaric process, then
explanation: When heat is supplied at constant pressure, a part of it goes in the expansion of gas and remaining part is used to increase the temperature of the gas which in turn increases the internal energy.

options.text: Internal energy of the gas increases
The work is done by the gas
Internal energy of the gas decreases

subject: Physics

question: Which of the following processes is reversible?
explanation: For process to be reversible it must be quasi-static. For quasi static process all changes take place very slowly. Isothermal process occur very slowly so it is quasi-static and hence it is reversible.

options.text: Electrical heating of a nichrome wire

question: Heat is added to an ideal gas and the gas expands. In such a process the temperature

options.text: Will remain the same if the work done equals the heat added
Will remain the same if change in internal energy equals the heat added

options.text: There will be energy loss due to friction
System and surroundings will not be in thermodynamic equilibrium
Both system and surroundings retain their initial states

options.text: Heat transfer is equal to work done.

explanation: Work is a path function so it depends on path.

options.text: The work done is independent of the path followed

question:
Match the Column I (type of processes) with Column II (feature).
A Isothermal
B Isobaric
C Isochoric
D Adiabatic
P $\Delta Q = 0$
Q Volume constant
R Pressure constant
S Temperature constant
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\rm{Type}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{processes}}}&{{\rm{Feature}}}\\ {{\rm{Isothermal}}}&{{\rm{Temperature}}\,\,{\rm{constant}}}\\ {{\rm{Isobaric}}}&{{\rm{Pressure}}\,{\rm{constant}}}\\ {{\rm{Isochoric}}}&{{\rm{Volume}}\,\,{\rm{constant}}}\\ {{\rm{Adiabatic}}}&{{\rm{\Delta Q = 0}}} \end{array}$
So options (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A monoatomic gas is compressed adiabatically till its temperature is doubled. The ratio of its final volume to initial volume will be:
explanation:
For adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned}& T_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma-1}=T_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma-1} \\& \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma-1}=2 \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{\gamma-1}=\dfrac{1}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}} < \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The gas law $\dfrac{P V}{T}=$ constant for a given gas is true for
subject: Physics

question: In an adiabatic compression the volume of an ideal gas decreases to ${\frac{1}{8}^{th}}$ of its initial value. Its final pressure becomes $\left(\gamma=\dfrac{5}{3}\right)$
explanation:
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
$P_{2}=P_{1}\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=P_{1}\left(\dfrac{V}{V / 8}\right)^{\gamma}=P_{1}\left(2^{3}\right)^{5 / 3}=32 P_{1}$
subject: Physics

question: For adiabatic processes $\left(\gamma=\dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}\right)$
explanation: In adiabatic process $P V^{\gamma}=$ constant $ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{RT}}{V}} \right) \cdot {V^\gamma } \Rightarrow T{V^{\gamma  - 1}} = $ constant.
subject: Physics

question: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature. The $\gamma$ of the gas is
explanation:
Given that
$P T^{-3}=$ constant$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For an adiabatic process,
$P T^{\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}}=$ constant
Comparing (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}=-3$ or $-3+3 \gamma=\gamma$
$2 \gamma=3$ or $\gamma=\dfrac{3}{2}=1.5$.
subject: Physics

question:
During an adiabatic expansion, the increase in volume is associated with which of the following possibilities regarding pressure and temperature ?
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{Pressure}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Temperature}}$
explanation:
$P{V^\gamma } = $ const $\Rightarrow V^{\gamma} \propto \dfrac{1}{P}$
$T V^{\gamma-1}=$ const $\Rightarrow V^{\gamma-1} \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$
subject: Physics

question: For an isothermal expansion of a perfect gas, the value of $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P}$ is equal to
explanation:
Differentiating $P V=$ constant w.r.t $V$
$\Rightarrow P \Delta V+V \Delta P=0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta P}{P}=-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$
subject: Physics

question: Air in a cylinder is suddenly compressed by a piston, which is then maintained at the same position. With the passage of time
explanation: Due to compression the temperature of the system increases to a very high value. This causes the flow of heat from system to the surroundings, thus decreasing the temperature. This decrease in temperature results in decrease in pressure.

options.text: The pressure may increase or decrease depending upon the nature of the gas

explanation: $W=n R T \ln \left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)$

options.text: Only on initial and final pressure
On both the temperature and volume expansion ratio

question: A gas is compressed isothermally. The rms velocity of its molecules
explanation:
The root mean square velocity of a gas molecule is given by
$v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 R T}{M}}$
Gas is compressed isothermally, so $T$ remains constant and hence, root mean square velocity will remain same
subject: Physics

question: Heat is supplied to a di-atomic gas at constant pressure. The ratio of $\Delta Q: \Delta U: \Delta W$ is :-
explanation:
$\dfrac{\Delta U}{\Delta Q}=\dfrac{n C_{V} \Delta T}{n C_{P} \Delta T}=\dfrac{1}{\gamma}=\dfrac{5}{7}$
$\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta Q}=\dfrac{\Delta Q-\Delta U}{\Delta Q}=1-\dfrac{1}{\gamma}=\dfrac{2}{7}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta Q: \Delta U: \Delta W=7: 5: 2$
subject: Physics

question: A gas expands under constant pressure $P$ from volume $V_{1}$ to $V_{2}$. The work done by the gas is
explanation: Work done $=P \Delta V=P\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: In which process the $PV$ indicator diagram is straight line parallel to volume axis
subject: Physics

question: Heat is absorbed by a body. But its temperature does not rise. Which of the following statement explains the phenomena?

options.text: Only P.E. of inter molecular force changes
No increase in internal energy takes place
Increase in K.E. is balanced by decrease in P.E.

subject: Physics

question: If $Q,\,\,E$ and $W$ denote respectively the heat added, change in internal energy and the work done in a closed cyclic process, then
explanation:
In a cyclic process, a system starts from one point and ends at the same point. In that case, the change in the internal energy must be zero and therefore, the thermal energy added to the system must be equal to the work done during the cycle, i.e., in a cyclic process,
$\Rightarrow \quad E=0$
subject: Physics

question: In a given process $dW = 0,\,\,dQ < 0$ then for a gas
explanation:
As $d W=0$ so it is an isochoric process $\dfrac{P}{T}=$ constant
Given $d q=d U < 0 \Rightarrow T$ decreases and hence $P$ also decreases.

options.text: It will depend upon the nature of the gas
Both will require the same amount of work
